Zeus negotiated her return, but Persephone had eaten six pomegranate seeds in the Underworld; by the laws of the realm, anyone who ate its food was bound to return. She now spends six months of the year in the Underworld as its queen, and six months in the upper world with her mother. When she descends, Demeter grieves and winter comes. When she returns, spring returns with her. Persephone's story explains the seasons through a divine custody arrangement.
facts.statement: Persephone became Queen of the Underworld after Hades abducted her — her annual return causes spring, and her descent causes winter.
facts.wowFactor: Six pomegranate seeds. That's what it cost Persephone six months of every year in the Underworld, and what it costs the earth six months of every year without spring. The Greeks explained the death and rebirth of the natural world as a mother's grief for her daughter, recurring every single year.
facts.visualDescription: A young queen on a dark underground throne, her expression poised between two worlds — the pale light of Elysium behind her and a faint thread of spring sunlight above, flowers growing from the stones at her feet

variants.question: Persephone must stay in the Underworld six months per year because she ate what?
Persephone's mother Demeter caused winter by doing what while searching for her daughter?
variants.correctAnswer: Neglecting the crops and letting all growth die

variants.question: True or false: Persephone spending six months in the Underworld each year is the Greek explanation for winter.
The Queen of the Underworld and daughter of Demeter was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which legendary musician descended to the Underworld to rescue his wife Eurydice, charming Hades himself with his lyre?
facts.explanation: Orpheus was the son of Apollo and the Muse Calliope (see myth_muses_calliope in this sub-deck), which made his gift not merely exceptional but divine in origin. When Eurydice died on their wedding day from a snakebite while fleeing the shepherd Aristaeus, Orpheus did something no living mortal had done willingly: he descended to the Underworld. His music silenced the punishments of Tartarus, made the Furies weep, and moved Hades — who had never granted mercy to anyone — to tears. Hades agreed: Eurydice could follow Orpheus back, as long as he did not look at her before they reached the surface. This is the same Underworld whose mechanics are described in the Underworld sub-deck; the River Styx, Charon, and Cerberus (bypassed by music here) all appear there in detail. Orpheus's story parallels the Norse Hermod's descent to Hel to retrieve Baldr — both heroes journey to the realm of the dead for love, both fail on the threshold by a single condition unmet.
facts.statement: Orpheus charmed every creature in the Underworld with his lyre — and won Hades's permission to take Eurydice back from death — then lost her with a single glance.
facts.wowFactor: Orpheus did something the gods of death considered literally impossible — he made Hades feel compassion. The god who had never once bent his law bent it for a musician's grief. The tragedy is not that Orpheus failed; it's that he succeeded at the impossible part and failed at the easy part.
facts.visualDescription: A narrow tunnel ascending through the earth, the walls still damp with the dark of the Underworld, two figures walking — the man ahead with a lyre on his back, the woman behind him translucent and almost real, both close enough that sunlight is beginning to show ahead — and his hand, just beginning to lift toward his shoulder to turn

variants.question: On what condition did Hades allow Orpheus to take Eurydice back to the living world?
variants.correctAnswer: He must not look back until they reached the surface
distractors: He must play music for Hades for a year
He must bring a golden offering

variants.question: How did Eurydice die, causing Orpheus to descend to the Underworld?
True or false: Orpheus successfully rescued Eurydice from the Underworld.
The musician who descended to the Underworld to save his wife Eurydice was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero visited the Underworld in Homer's Odyssey, speaking with the shades of heroes and receiving prophecy from Tiresias?
facts.explanation: In Book XI of the Odyssey — the Nekyia — Odysseus traveled to the boundary of the Underworld and performed a ritual: he dug a trench, poured libations, and sacrificed animals so that the shades of the dead would be drawn to the blood. Tiresias gave him prophecy about the journey ahead. His mother Anticleia told him about his household — the suitors, Penelope's faithfulness, his father's grief. Achilles, resting in Elysium, told him famously that he would rather be a living slave than king of all the dead. Ajax refused to speak to him, still furious about the armor judgment. He saw Sisyphus, Tantalus, and Heracles. Then the crowd of shades grew too great and he retreated to his ship. The living man had peered into the land of the dead — and returned with knowledge no one else had.
facts.statement: Odysseus visited the edge of the Underworld in the Odyssey — he did not enter but called the shades to him with blood, speaking with Tiresias, Achilles, Ajax, and his own mother.
facts.wowFactor: Achilles, who chose eternal glory over a long life, told Odysseus from Elysium that he'd trade everything — the glory, the fame, the hero's rest — to be the lowest living farmhand. The man who chose to die young and be remembered forever would rather just be alive.
facts.visualDescription: An armored hero at a dark trench of sacrifice-blood, shades pressing from the mist — a blind prophet with his staff, a translucent woman reaching toward him, a great hero in the distance keeping his back turned in silence

variants.question: In Homer's Odyssey, what prophet did Odysseus travel to the Underworld to consult?
What did Odysseus use to summon the shades of the dead in the Odyssey's Underworld visit?
True or false: In the Odyssey, Odysseus fully entered the Underworld realm to speak with the dead.
The hero who visited the Underworld in Homer's Odyssey to consult the prophet Tiresias was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess drove Heracles mad, causing him to kill his own family and triggering the Twelve Labors?
facts.explanation: Hera hated Heracles from birth because he was Zeus's illegitimate son. She sent a fit of madness upon him that caused him to murder his wife Megara and their children. When Heracles recovered and realized what he had done, he consulted the Oracle at Delphi, who commanded him to perform twelve labors as penance — serving King Eurystheus of Mycenae.
facts.statement: Hera drove Heracles mad, leading him to kill his family and triggering the Twelve Labors.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest hero in Greek mythology committed his most heroic acts as punishment for crimes he committed while literally insane — driven mad by a jealous goddess who never forgave him for being born.
facts.visualDescription: A muscular hero in anguish surrounded by divine golden light and storm clouds, a goddess figure watching from above with cold fury

variants.question: Heracles performed the Twelve Labors as penance for what crime?
variants.correctAnswer: Killing his wife and children while driven mad

variants.question: Which king did the Oracle at Delphi command Heracles to serve when performing the Twelve Labors?
True or false: Heracles volunteered to perform the Twelve Labors to prove his strength.
The goddess who drove Heracles mad, causing him to kill his family, was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the first labor of Heracles — a beast with impenetrable golden fur that he strangled with his bare hands?
facts.explanation: The first of Heracles' twelve labors was to slay the Nemean Lion, a monstrous beast whose golden hide could not be pierced by any weapon. Heracles drove it into a cave, blocked one entrance, and strangled it to death with his bare hands. He then used the lion's own claws — the only thing sharp enough — to skin it. He wore the pelt as his iconic armor for the rest of his life.
facts.statement: Heracles strangled the Nemean Lion for his first labor and wore its impenetrable hide as armor.
facts.wowFactor: No weapon could pierce the Nemean Lion's skin — so Heracles had to kill it with his hands. He then used the lion's own claws to skin it, and wore the hide as armor that no blade could cut.
facts.visualDescription: Powerful hero wrestling a massive golden lion in rocky terrain, bare hands gripping its massive neck, the beast's golden fur glowing

variants.question: How did Heracles kill the Nemean Lion, whose hide no weapon could pierce?
variants.correctAnswer: He strangled it with his bare hands
distractors: He trapped it in a net
He starved it in a cave

variants.question: What did Heracles do with the Nemean Lion's hide after killing it?
distractors: He gave it to King Eurystheus
He draped it over the Oracle's throne
He burned it as an offering to Zeus

variants.question: True or false: The Nemean Lion's hide was invulnerable to all weapons.
Heracles' first labor was to slay the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: In Heracles' second labor, which multi-headed serpent regrew two heads every time one was cut off?
facts.explanation: The Lernaean Hydra was a monstrous water serpent with multiple heads — when any head was cut off, two grew back in its place. Heracles enlisted his nephew Iolaus to cauterize each neck stump with a burning torch immediately after cutting, preventing regrowth. He then dipped his arrows in the Hydra's poisonous blood, which he later used to deadly effect — including accidentally causing his own death.
facts.statement: The Hydra regrew two heads for every one cut off; Heracles and Iolaus defeated it by cauterizing each stump with fire.
facts.wowFactor: Every time Heracles cut off a head, two more grew back — the ultimate unwinnable fight. His nephew Iolaus's idea to burn each stump before they could regenerate was what actually solved it. The Hydra's venom would later kill Heracles himself.
facts.visualDescription: Massive multi-headed serpent rising from a dark swamp, each neck spurting blood, a hero slashing while a young man with a torch cauterizes the stumps

variants.question: How did Heracles and Iolaus prevent the Hydra's heads from regrowing?
variants.correctAnswer: By cauterizing each stump with fire
distractors: By using the Nemean Lion's claws to sear the wounds
By submerging the heads in the Styx

variants.question: Who helped Heracles defeat the Hydra by burning the neck stumps?
True or false: Cutting off one of the Hydra's heads caused two more to grow back.
Heracles defeated the Lernaean ___ with the help of his nephew Iolaus.

facts.quizQuestion: In his fifth labor, how did Heracles clean the Augean Stables — 30 years of accumulated filth from 3,000 divine cattle — in a single day?
facts.explanation: King Augeas owned 3,000 divine cattle, and their stables had not been cleaned in thirty years. Eurystheus set this as a labor he believed impossible in a single day. Heracles solved it by engineering a solution: he redirected two rivers — the Alpheus and the Peneus — to flow directly through the stables, flushing out all the filth in one day. Augeas had promised Heracles a tenth of his cattle as payment but refused to pay, leading to later conflict.
facts.statement: Heracles cleaned the Augean Stables in one day by redirecting two rivers through them.
facts.wowFactor: Heracles didn't just lift boulders — he was also an engineer. He solved a seemingly impossible sanitation problem for 3,000 cattle by rerouting rivers. The greatest strongman of antiquity was also a civil engineer.
facts.visualDescription: A muscular hero redirecting a massive river with bare hands, stinking stables in the background, torrents of water flooding through wooden gates

variants.question: What made the Augean Stables so impossible to clean by conventional means?
variants.correctAnswer: 30 years of waste from 3,000 divine cattle
distractors: The cattle were immortal and never stopped producing
Poseidon had flooded the stables with seawater
The filth had been magically enchanted

variants.question: Which two rivers did Heracles redirect through the Augean Stables?
True or false: Heracles cleaned the Augean Stables by redirecting rivers through them in a single day.
Heracles cleaned the Augean Stables for his fifth labor by redirecting ___ through them.

facts.quizQuestion: Which man-eating bronze-feathered birds did Heracles drive away for his sixth labor using noise-making instruments given by Athena?
facts.explanation: The Stymphalian Birds were monstrous man-eating birds with bronze beaks, wings, and claws that infested the forests around Lake Stymphalia in Arcadia. They ruined crops and killed people. Athena gave Heracles a pair of krotala (bronze castanets or clappers made by Hephaestus). He climbed a nearby mountain and shook them, startling the birds into flight, then shot them with his arrows or drove them away to the Black Sea.
facts.statement: Heracles drove away the man-eating Stymphalian Birds using bronze castanets given by Athena.
facts.wowFactor: The solution to man-eating bronze birds wasn't brute force — it was making enough noise to scare them off. Athena had Hephaestus forge a giant rattle just for this purpose. Heracles essentially won this labor by being loud.
facts.visualDescription: Bronze-feathered birds with metallic wings darkening the sky, a hero on a mountaintop shaking rattling instruments, arrows flying upward into a flock

variants.question: What made the Stymphalian Birds unusual among creatures Heracles had to deal with?
variants.correctAnswer: They had bronze beaks, feathers, and claws and ate humans
distractors: They were immortal and could not be killed
They could only be harmed by fire
They were sacred to Ares and protected by a curse

variants.question: Who gave Heracles the bronze noise-makers to startle the Stymphalian Birds?
True or false: The Stymphalian Birds were man-eating creatures with bronze feathers and beaks.
Heracles' sixth labor involved driving away the man-eating ___ using bronze castanets.

facts.quizQuestion: Whose magic girdle did Heracles retrieve for his ninth labor, a gift from Ares to the queen of the warrior women?
facts.explanation: Heracles' ninth labor was to obtain the magical belt (girdle) of Hippolyta, queen of the Amazons — a warrior belt given to her by Ares, the god of war. Hippolyta was initially willing to give it peacefully, but Hera (still persecuting Heracles) disguised herself as an Amazon and spread rumors that Heracles had come to kidnap the queen. In the resulting battle, Heracles killed Hippolyta and took the belt.
facts.statement: Heracles retrieved the magical belt of Hippolyta, queen of the Amazons, for his ninth labor.
facts.wowFactor: Hippolyta was going to hand the belt over peacefully — Heracles had actually impressed her. But Hera disguised herself and convinced the Amazons he was there to kidnap their queen, turning a diplomatic success into a bloody battle.
facts.visualDescription: Armored Amazon queen with a glowing golden belt, surrounded by warrior women, a hero approaching under a tense sky at the edge of a great encampment

variants.question: Who gave Hippolyta her magical belt that Heracles was sent to retrieve?
Why did the battle with the Amazons occur despite Hippolyta's initial willingness to cooperate?
variants.correctAnswer: Hera disguised herself and spread false rumors
distractors: Heracles accidentally killed an Amazon warrior first
Eurystheus ordered Heracles to take it by force
An oracle warned the Amazons not to give up the belt
Hippolyta changed her mind after consulting Ares

variants.question: True or false: Hippolyta was the queen of the Amazons and her belt was a gift from Ares.
The Amazon queen whose belt Heracles seized for his ninth labor was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: To get the golden apples from the garden guarded by Ladon the dragon, which Titan did Heracles trick into fetching them by briefly holding up the sky?
facts.explanation: The eleventh labor sent Heracles to steal the golden apples from the Garden of the Hesperides, protected by the dragon Ladon and the Hesperide nymphs. Heracles found Atlas — the Titan condemned to hold up the sky forever — and offered to hold the heavens while Atlas fetched the apples. Atlas did so, but then refused to take the sky back. Heracles tricked him by asking Atlas to hold it briefly while he adjusted his cloak, then walked away with the apples.
facts.statement: Heracles tricked Atlas into fetching the golden apples by temporarily holding up the sky for him.
facts.wowFactor: Heracles held up the entire sky — the literal weight of the heavens — so Atlas could get some apples. Then Atlas tried to keep him stuck there forever. Heracles escaped by pretending to need a shoulder pad, and Atlas fell for it.
facts.visualDescription: A hero straining under the massive weight of the sky, a huge titan figure in the distance carrying golden fruit, a dragon coiled around a luminous tree

variants.question: What dragon guarded the golden apples in the Garden of the Hesperides?
How did Heracles trick Atlas into taking the sky back after getting the golden apples?
variants.correctAnswer: He asked Atlas to hold it briefly while he adjusted a cushion, then walked away
distractors: He threatened Atlas with his club
He promised to return with three more apples
He said Zeus had commanded Atlas to resume his burden
He told Atlas Eurystheus demanded both the apples and the Titan

variants.question: True or false: Heracles personally slew Ladon the dragon to get the golden apples.
To obtain the golden apples, Heracles temporarily held up the sky for the Titan ___.

facts.quizQuestion: What three-headed dog guarding the Underworld did Heracles capture for his twelfth and final labor, returning it to Eurystheus before releasing it?
facts.explanation: The twelfth and final labor was to bring Cerberus — the three-headed dog that guarded the gates of the Underworld — up from Hades. Heracles descended into the Underworld, where Hades permitted him to take Cerberus on one condition: he must subdue the beast with his bare hands, no weapons. Heracles wrestled Cerberus into submission, dragged him to Eurystheus (who hid in a jar in terror), then returned the dog to Hades.
facts.statement: Heracles captured Cerberus from the Underworld with bare hands for his final labor, then returned it.
facts.wowFactor: King Eurystheus — who had tormented Heracles with impossible tasks for years — was so terrified when Heracles showed up with a three-headed hell-hound that he hid inside a large bronze storage jar. The most feared king in Greece hid in a pot.
facts.visualDescription: A hero wrestling a massive three-headed black dog with a serpentine tail, glowing red eyes, deep cavern with bones and shadows of the dead watching

variants.question: What was Hades' one condition for allowing Heracles to take Cerberus from the Underworld?
variants.correctAnswer: Heracles must subdue him with bare hands, no weapons
distractors: Heracles must bring back a soul from Elysium
Heracles must return within one day
Heracles must leave his lion skin behind
Heracles must promise never to return to the Underworld

variants.question: How did King Eurystheus react when Heracles returned with Cerberus?
variants.correctAnswer: He hid inside a large bronze jar in terror
distractors: He commanded the guards to slay the beast
He immediately sent Heracles on a thirteenth labor
He released Cerberus back to the Underworld himself

variants.question: True or false: Cerberus was the three-headed dog that guarded the entrance to the Underworld.
Heracles' twelfth and final labor was to capture ___ from the Underworld.

facts.quizQuestion: Which centaur's poisoned blood, smeared on a robe by a deceived wife, caused the death of Heracles?
facts.explanation: Nessus was a centaur Heracles killed while crossing a river — Nessus had tried to assault Heracles' wife Deianira. Dying, Nessus tricked Deianira: he told her to collect his blood as a 'love potion' that would keep Heracles faithful. Years later, when Deianira feared Heracles was falling for another woman, she smeared his robe with Nessus's blood. It was actually the Hydra's poison — the same Heracles had used on his arrows. The robe burned Heracles alive.
facts.statement: The centaur Nessus tricked Deianira into poisoning Heracles with a robe soaked in Hydra-tainted blood.
facts.wowFactor: The man who slew the Hydra, strangled the Nemean Lion, and wrestled Cerberus was killed by his own wife — who was herself tricked by a centaur he had shot with an arrow tipped in the same poison. The arrow that killed the centaur ultimately killed Heracles.
facts.visualDescription: A dying centaur whispering to a tearful woman beside a river, his hand pressing a vial of dark blood into hers, arrows sticking from his flank

variants.question: What was the true nature of the 'love potion' the centaur Nessus gave to Deianira?
variants.correctAnswer: The Hydra's poison mixed with his blood
distractors: A divine perfume from Aphrodite's temple
Ground mandrake root soaked in river water
Cursed blood of a Stymphalian Bird

variants.question: Who was Heracles' wife who unknowingly caused his death?
True or false: Heracles was killed by a robe soaked in the centaur Nessus's poisoned blood.
The shirt of ___, soaked in Hydra-tainted blood, burned Heracles alive and caused his death.

facts.quizQuestion: After Heracles died on his funeral pyre, which god brought his immortal soul to Olympus, where he became a god and reconciled with Hera?
facts.explanation: Heracles, in agony from the poisoned robe, had himself placed on a great funeral pyre on Mount Oeta and commanded it to be lit. As his mortal body burned away, Zeus sent down lightning to carry his immortal soul to Olympus. There, Heracles was made a god, married Hebe (the goddess of youth), and remarkably reconciled with Hera. The man who suffered his entire life because of Hera's hatred became her son-in-law in death.
facts.statement: After his death on a funeral pyre, Zeus brought Heracles to Olympus where he became a god and married Hebe.
facts.wowFactor: Hera spent Heracles' entire life trying to destroy him — and after he died and became a god, she gave him her daughter Hebe as a wife and they reconciled. The greatest hero's story ends with a family reunion with his worst enemy.
facts.visualDescription: A hero's soul ascending in golden light from a blazing mountaintop pyre, Olympian gods watching above the clouds, a goddess figure extending a hand in welcome

variants.question: Whom did Heracles marry after becoming a god on Olympus?
On which mountain did Heracles build his own funeral pyre before his apotheosis?
True or false: After Heracles died and became a god, he reconciled with Hera, who had persecuted him throughout his life.
After dying in agony, Heracles' soul was taken to Olympus by ___ and he became a god.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god visited the imprisoned Danae as a shower of golden rain, fathering the hero Perseus?
facts.explanation: King Acrisius of Argos was warned by a prophecy that his grandson would kill him, so he locked his daughter Danae in a bronze chamber underground (or a bronze tower, in some versions). Zeus, smitten with Danae, transformed himself into a shower of golden rain and visited her through the ceiling. Perseus was conceived. When Acrisius discovered the baby, he locked Danae and Perseus in a chest and cast them into the sea.
facts.statement: Zeus visited the imprisoned Danae as golden rain, fathering Perseus; King Acrisius then cast mother and child adrift at sea.
facts.wowFactor: Perseus's grandfather tried to prevent the prophecy by locking his daughter in a bronze vault — and Zeus turned himself into rain to get inside anyway. The grandfather then threw them into the sea rather than killing them directly, and that mercy sealed his fate.
facts.visualDescription: Golden rain streaming through a bronze grate into a dimly lit chamber, a woman looking upward in wonder, divine light filling the underground cell

variants.question: Why did King Acrisius imprison his daughter Danae in a bronze chamber?
variants.correctAnswer: A prophecy said her son would kill him
distractors: She had dishonored the temple of Hera
She had rejected the advances of Poseidon
She had stolen sacred gold from Delphi
She was being punished for loving a mortal

variants.question: What did King Acrisius do with Danae and the infant Perseus when he found out about the birth?
variants.correctAnswer: He sealed them in a chest and cast them into the sea
distractors: He killed Perseus but spared Danae
He sent them to live in Crete
He gave them to Poseidon as an offering
He locked them in a deeper underground vault

variants.question: True or false: Zeus visited Danae by transforming into a shower of golden rain.
Perseus was born to Danae after she was visited by ___ in the form of golden rain.

facts.quizQuestion: Which king sent Perseus on the seemingly impossible quest to fetch Medusa's head, hoping to be rid of the young hero?
facts.explanation: Danae and Perseus washed ashore on the island of Seriphos, where King Polydectes fell in love with Danae. Perseus protected his mother from the king's advances. Polydectes devised a trap: at a banquet, he demanded each guest bring a horse as a gift — except Perseus, whom he challenged to bring the head of Medusa the Gorgon instead, a task he expected would kill the hero. With help from Athena and Hermes, Perseus succeeded.
facts.statement: King Polydectes sent Perseus to fetch Medusa's head, hoping it would get him killed so he could pursue Danae.
facts.wowFactor: Polydectes didn't want Medusa's head — he wanted Perseus dead so he could force himself on Perseus's mother. The greatest monster-slaying quest in Greek myth started as a jealous king's murder plot.
facts.visualDescription: An arrogant king on a throne gesturing dismissively toward a defiant young hero, a court full of nobles with fine horses, tension crackling in a marble hall

variants.question: Why did King Polydectes send Perseus to slay Medusa?
variants.correctAnswer: He wanted Perseus dead so he could pursue Danae
distractors: He genuinely needed Medusa's head to break a curse
He was testing Perseus's worthiness to marry his daughter
An oracle demanded Medusa's head as tribute to Poseidon
He feared Medusa would destroy his kingdom

variants.question: On which island did Danae and Perseus land after being cast adrift by King Acrisius?
True or false: King Polydectes sent Perseus to kill Medusa because he was genuinely afraid of her.
Perseus was sent to fetch Medusa's head by the scheming King ___ of Seriphos.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god gave Perseus winged sandals, a kibisis (magical bag), and an adamantine sword to aid his quest against Medusa?
facts.explanation: Perseus received divine gifts from multiple gods for his quest. Athena gave him a mirrored bronze shield so he could look at Medusa's reflection rather than directly (which would petrify him). Hermes gave him winged sandals for flight and an adamantine sword. He also obtained Hades' cap of invisibility and a magical kibisis (bag) from nymphs. Together, these gifts made the impossible quest possible.
facts.statement: Hermes gave Perseus winged sandals and a sword; Athena gave a mirrored shield; Hades' cap of invisibility rounded out his divine toolkit.
facts.wowFactor: Perseus went into his monster-slaying mission with equipment sourced from three different gods and the nymphs — Athena's mirror shield, Hermes' flying sandals, a sword that could cut through anything, and an invisibility cap. He was the best-equipped hero in Greek myth.
facts.visualDescription: A young hero receiving glowing gifts from divine figures — a mirrored shield, winged sandals, a glittering sword — in a cloud-filled heavenly setting

variants.question: What did Athena give Perseus to help him face Medusa without being petrified?
variants.correctAnswer: A mirrored bronze shield to see Medusa's reflection
distractors: A blindfold woven from divine thread
Earplugs so he wouldn't hear Medusa's cry
Winged sandals to stay out of Medusa's reach
A helmet that let him see through walls

variants.question: Which god's cap of invisibility did Perseus use during his quest to kill Medusa?
True or false: Athena gave Perseus a mirrored shield so he could look at Medusa's reflection instead of directly at her.
Perseus received winged sandals and a sword from the god ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Gorgon did Perseus behead while looking only at her reflection — and from whose blood sprang the winged horse Pegasus?
facts.explanation: Perseus used Athena's mirrored shield to approach Medusa without looking directly at her (which would turn him to stone). He beheaded her with one stroke of his adamantine sword. From Medusa's severed neck, two beings sprang forth: the winged horse Pegasus and the giant Chrysaor. Perseus put the head in his magic bag — it still retained the power to petrify — and used it as a weapon multiple times on his journey home.
facts.statement: Perseus beheaded Medusa using a mirrored shield; from her blood sprang Pegasus the winged horse.
facts.wowFactor: Medusa's death wasn't the end of her power — her severed head could still petrify anyone who looked at it. Perseus essentially carried a weapon of mass destruction in a bag all the way home and used it to turn his enemies to stone.
facts.visualDescription: A hero averting his eyes while looking at a mirrored shield, a snake-haired figure frozen mid-scream below him, a white winged horse bursting from blood in the background

variants.question: What two beings sprang from Medusa's blood when Perseus beheaded her?
distractors: Two new Gorgons to replace her
The Graeae and a sea serpent

variants.question: How did Perseus approach Medusa without being turned to stone?
variants.correctAnswer: He looked only at her reflection in Athena's mirrored shield
distractors: He wore Hades' cap of invisibility the entire time
He kept his eyes closed and guided by sound
He wore a blindfold that let him sense heat instead of seeing
He moved at superhuman speed so the gaze had no time to work

variants.question: True or false: The winged horse Pegasus was born from Medusa's blood when Perseus cut off her head.
Perseus beheaded ___ while looking at her reflection, and the winged horse Pegasus was born from her blood.

facts.quizQuestion: Which princess chained to a rock as a sacrifice to a sea monster did Perseus rescue — using Medusa's head to petrify the beast?
facts.explanation: On his return journey, Perseus spotted a princess chained to a sea cliff: Andromeda, daughter of King Cepheus and Queen Cassiopeia of Ethiopia. Cassiopeia had boasted her daughter was more beautiful than the Nereids, angering Poseidon, who sent a sea monster (Cetus) to ravage the coast. The only remedy, per an oracle, was to sacrifice Andromeda. Perseus killed the monster using Medusa's head to petrify it, freed Andromeda, and married her.
facts.statement: Perseus rescued Princess Andromeda from a sea monster by petrifying it with Medusa's severed head.
facts.wowFactor: Perseus was flying home after decapitating a monster when he spotted a princess chained to a cliff as monster bait. He stopped, petrified the sea monster with a severed head he happened to be carrying, and married the princess — all before landing.
facts.visualDescription: A princess chained to rocky sea cliffs, a massive sea serpent rising from the waves, a hero flying on winged sandals wielding a glowing severed head toward the beast

variants.question: Why was Princess Andromeda chained to a rock as a sacrifice?
variants.correctAnswer: Her mother Cassiopeia boasted she was more beautiful than the Nereids, angering Poseidon
distractors: She had stolen a golden fleece from Poseidon's temple
A prophecy demanded a royal sacrifice to calm a storm
She had rejected a sea god's advances
Her father Cepheus had insulted Ares in battle

variants.question: What was the name of the sea monster Poseidon sent that Andromeda was sacrificed to appease?
True or false: Perseus used Medusa's severed head to turn the sea monster threatening Andromeda into stone.
Perseus rescued the princess ___ from a sea monster and married her.

facts.quizQuestion: Which king — Perseus's own grandfather — died when an errant discus thrown at athletic games struck him, fulfilling the prophecy he had tried so hard to prevent?
facts.explanation: After his adventures, Perseus returned to Greece and founded Mycenae. His grandfather Acrisius, terrified the prophecy would come true, had fled to Larissa. Perseus later competed in athletic games in Larissa — by coincidence or fate — and during a discus throw, a gust of wind sent the discus into the crowd, striking and killing Acrisius. The prophecy was fulfilled not through murder but through tragic accident. Perseus was grief-stricken and gave up the kingdom of Argos.
facts.statement: Perseus accidentally killed his grandfather Acrisius with a discus at athletic games, fulfilling the prophecy.
facts.wowFactor: Acrisius tried everything to stop the prophecy: locked his daughter away, threw her and her baby into the sea, fled to another city. The prophecy found him anyway through a discus accident at a sporting event Perseus happened to attend. Fate wins every time.
facts.visualDescription: A stone discus spinning through a crowd at ancient athletic games, sunlight catching it in mid-arc, horror on spectators' faces as it veers toward an old king

variants.question: What city did Perseus found after his heroic adventures?
How did Perseus accidentally kill his grandfather Acrisius?
variants.correctAnswer: An errant discus throw struck him at athletic games
distractors: He tripped and fell onto Acrisius's sword during a reunion
Perseus accidentally showed him Medusa's head
He crashed into Acrisius while landing from his winged sandals
A chariot race went wrong and the vehicle struck the king

variants.question: True or false: Perseus intentionally killed his grandfather Acrisius to fulfill the prophecy.
The prophecy that King ___ had tried his whole life to prevent was finally fulfilled when an accidental discus throw killed him.

facts.quizQuestion: In which river did Thetis dip the infant Achilles to make him invulnerable, holding him by the heel that became his fatal weakness?
facts.explanation: The sea-nymph Thetis, knowing her son Achilles was fated to die young, tried to make him immortal by dipping him in the River Styx — the boundary between the living world and the Underworld. The waters of the Styx granted invulnerability to whatever they touched. But Thetis held him by his heel, which never touched the water, leaving that one spot vulnerable — a vulnerability that would eventually kill him.
facts.statement: Thetis dipped Achilles in the River Styx to make him invulnerable, but the heel she held remained his only weak point.
facts.wowFactor: The most famous vulnerability in all mythology came from a logistical problem: how do you dip a baby in a river without holding it? Thetis didn't think that part through, and it cost her son his life.
facts.visualDescription: A divine sea-nymph holding an infant over dark glowing waters in a cavernous underworld setting, the water touching everything except the tiny heel in her grip

variants.question: Why was Achilles' heel the only vulnerable part of his body?
variants.correctAnswer: His mother held him there when dipping him in the Styx, so it never touched the water
distractors: A curse from Hera targeted that specific spot
He was struck by Apollo's arrow at birth
A Trojan oracle identified the heel as his fate-bound weak point
He never completed the ritual — only half his body was dipped

variants.question: Who was Achilles' mother, the sea-nymph who tried to make him invulnerable?
True or false: Thetis dipped Achilles in the River Styx to grant him invulnerability everywhere except his heel.
Thetis made Achilles nearly invulnerable by dipping him in the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Greek hero, considered the greatest warrior at Troy, commanded the elite Myrmidon soldiers throughout the siege?
facts.explanation: Achilles was the greatest warrior among the Greeks at Troy — faster and stronger than any mortal. He commanded the Myrmidons, an elite force of warriors from Phthia in Thessaly. Homer's Iliad centers almost entirely on Achilles' rage and its consequences. Without Achilles, the Greeks could not win; yet his pride and grief repeatedly withdrew him from the battlefield at critical moments.
facts.statement: Achilles, the greatest Greek warrior at Troy, commanded the elite Myrmidon soldiers.
facts.wowFactor: The Iliad — the foundational text of Western literature — is not really about the Trojan War. It's about Achilles' anger for nine days. Everything else — ten years of war — is backdrop for one warrior's emotional state.
facts.visualDescription: A gleaming warrior in divine armor standing before a formation of elite soldiers, Troy's walls burning in the distant background, an aura of invincibility about him

variants.question: What elite warrior force did Achilles command at the Trojan War?
Homer's Iliad focuses primarily on the consequences of whose rage during the Trojan War?
True or false: Achilles was considered the greatest Greek warrior at the siege of Troy.
The greatest Greek warrior at Troy, who led the Myrmidons, was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Greek king of kings took Achilles' war-prize Briseis, triggering Achilles' withdrawal from battle and setting the Iliad in motion?
facts.explanation: Agamemnon, commander of the Greek forces at Troy, was forced to return his own captive Chryseis to her father (a priest of Apollo) or face divine plague. Furious at the loss, Agamemnon seized Briseis — a captive Achilles had won in battle — as replacement. Achilles, enraged at this dishonor, withdrew himself and his Myrmidons entirely from the war. Without their greatest warrior, the Greeks suffered terrible losses, directly driving the plot of the Iliad.
facts.statement: Agamemnon took Achilles' captive Briseis, causing Achilles to withdraw from battle and setting the Trojan War's crisis in motion.
facts.wowFactor: The entire plot of the Iliad — thousands of Greek deaths, Patroclus's death, Hector's death — traces back to a king taking another man's trophy and a warrior's wounded pride. The war's biggest crisis was about honor, not strategy.
facts.visualDescription: A proud king gesturing imperiously toward a captive woman, a furious armored warrior clenching his fist and turning away, a tent camp with warships behind them

variants.question: Why did Agamemnon take Briseis from Achilles?
variants.correctAnswer: Apollo's plague forced him to return his own captive, so he took Achilles' as replacement
distractors: He claimed all captured women belonged to the commander by tradition
Hera commanded him to humiliate Achilles
He needed to trade Briseis to Priam for peace negotiations
An oracle said Briseis held the key to Troy's defeat

variants.question: What happened to the Greek army after Achilles withdrew from battle?
variants.correctAnswer: They suffered serious losses without their greatest warrior
distractors: They immediately broke through Troy's walls without him
Zeus sent storms that destroyed their fleet
Odysseus took command and won a major victory
The Trojans negotiated a temporary truce

variants.question: True or false: Achilles withdrew from the Trojan War after Agamemnon took his captive Briseis.
Achilles refused to fight after ___ seized his war-prize Briseis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which beloved companion of Achilles put on his armor to rally the Greeks, but was killed by Hector — finally bringing Achilles back into the war in rage?
facts.explanation: Patroclus was Achilles' closest companion and possibly his lover. Seeing the Greeks losing badly while Achilles sulked, Patroclus begged to borrow Achilles' divine armor to rally the troops. Achilles agreed but warned him not to pursue the Trojans to their walls. Patroclus drove the Trojans back but pressed too far — Apollo stunned him and Hector killed him, stripping the armor. Achilles' grief and rage at Patroclus's death brought him back to war.
facts.statement: Patroclus wore Achilles' armor to rally the Greeks but was killed by Hector, finally driving Achilles back into battle.
facts.wowFactor: The death that changed the entire war was not a great hero cut down in battle — it was a man wearing someone else's armor, borrowing someone else's glory, who got too bold and paid for it. Patroclus's death mattered because of what it did to Achilles.
facts.visualDescription: A warrior in blazing borrowed armor falling from a chariot as a Trojan prince stands victorious above him, divine light fading from the armor as it's stripped away

variants.question: What warning did Achilles give Patroclus before lending him his armor?
variants.correctAnswer: Not to chase the Trojans back to their walls
distractors: Not to remove the helmet in battle
To return before sunset or the armor's power would fade
Not to invoke the gods' names during combat

variants.question: Who struck Patroclus first, stunning him before Hector landed the killing blow?
True or false: Patroclus was killed by Hector while wearing Achilles' armor.
Achilles' closest companion ___ borrowed his armor and was slain by Hector.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Trojan champion did Achilles kill in single combat and then drag around the walls of Troy behind his chariot in grief-fueled rage?
facts.explanation: Maddened with grief and rage over Patroclus's death, Achilles donned new divine armor forged by Hephaestus and re-entered the war. He hunted down Hector, prince of Troy and its greatest warrior. They fought in single combat outside Troy's walls. Achilles killed him and then, rather than returning the body for burial rites, tied it to his chariot and dragged it around the city walls in furious humiliation — a profound desecration in Greek culture. Hector's father King Priam eventually came to beg for the body.
facts.statement: Achilles killed Hector in single combat and dragged his body around Troy's walls in grief-fueled desecration.
facts.wowFactor: After killing Hector, Achilles dragged the corpse around Troy's walls daily for days — in front of Hector's wife, parents, and the entire city. King Priam, Troy's aged father-king, then walked alone into the Greek camp at night to beg for his son's body. It's one of the most moving scenes in all of Homer.
facts.visualDescription: A raging warrior lashing a defeated hero's body to a chariot, stone city walls looming above, Trojans watching in horror from the battlements

variants.question: Who eventually convinced Achilles to return Hector's body for proper burial?
variants.correctAnswer: King Priam of Troy, Hector's father
distractors: The goddess Athena appearing in disguise
A vision of Patroclus commanding it in a dream

variants.question: Who forged Achilles' new divine armor after Hector stripped the original set from Patroclus?
True or false: After killing Hector, Achilles dragged his body around the walls of Troy behind a chariot.
Achilles killed Troy's greatest warrior ___ in single combat to avenge Patroclus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Trojan prince shot the arrow guided by Apollo that struck Achilles in his only vulnerable spot — his heel?
facts.explanation: Achilles' death was prophesied: he would die young at Troy but win eternal glory. The arrow that killed him was shot by Paris — prince of Troy, lover of Helen, and a far better archer than swordsman. Apollo guided the arrow to its mark, striking Achilles in his vulnerable heel. The greatest warrior of the age, invulnerable everywhere else, died from a wound to his one unprotected spot — shot by a man widely considered no great warrior himself.
facts.statement: Paris shot an arrow guided by Apollo that struck Achilles in his heel — his only vulnerable spot — killing the greatest Greek warrior.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest warrior in the world, literally invulnerable everywhere except his heel, was killed by the guy who started the whole war and couldn't fight his way out of a duel without a god's help. The universe has a dark sense of irony.
facts.visualDescription: A Trojan archer on the city walls loosing an arrow guided by a divine glow, the arrow curving toward a hero's heel as he charges below, golden light illuminating the fatal trajectory

variants.question: Which god guided Paris's arrow to Achilles' heel?
What was the irony of Paris being the one to kill Achilles?
variants.correctAnswer: Paris was considered an inferior warrior who relied on archery rather than fighting
distractors: Paris and Achilles had been childhood friends
Paris was supposed to die first according to the prophecy
Paris was fighting in armor belonging to Hector
Apollo had previously protected Achilles from Paris's arrows

variants.question: True or false: Achilles was killed by an arrow to the heel shot by Paris and guided by Apollo.
The Trojan prince ___ shot the arrow — guided by Apollo — that killed Achilles.

facts.quizQuestion: What bull-headed monster living in the Labyrinth of Crete did Theseus volunteer to enter and kill, ending Athens's tribute of seven youths and seven maidens every nine years?
facts.explanation: Athens was forced by King Minos of Crete to send a tribute of seven young men and seven young women every nine years as food for the Minotaur — a half-man, half-bull monster born to Minos's wife Pasiphae, confined in the Labyrinth designed by Daedalus. Theseus, son of the Athenian king Aegeus, volunteered to go as one of the tribute-youths, intending to kill the Minotaur and end the tribute forever.
facts.statement: Theseus volunteered to enter the Labyrinth and kill the Minotaur, ending Athens's human tribute to Crete.
facts.wowFactor: Athens paid tribute in human children to be eaten by a monster. For an unknown number of generations. Theseus volunteered to be one of those children, with the secret plan to just kill the monster from the inside. It was essentially a one-man special forces mission.
facts.visualDescription: A determined young prince among a group of frightened youths boarding a black-sailed ship, stone city walls behind them, the sea stretching toward a distant island

variants.question: How often did Athens have to send tribute of seven young men and seven young women to Crete?
Who designed the Labyrinth that contained the Minotaur on Crete?
True or false: The Minotaur was half-man and half-bull, confined in the Labyrinth of Crete.
Theseus volunteered to enter the Labyrinth and kill the ___, ending Athens's tribute of human youths.

facts.quizQuestion: Which daughter of King Minos fell in love with Theseus and gave him a ball of thread to find his way out of the Labyrinth?
facts.explanation: Ariadne, daughter of King Minos and Princess of Crete, fell in love with Theseus when she saw him among the Athenian tribute. She went to Daedalus (who had designed the Labyrinth) and learned its secret. She then gave Theseus a ball of thread — which he tied to the entrance and unrolled as he walked — allowing him to retrace his steps after killing the Minotaur. Without Ariadne's thread, escaping the Labyrinth would have been as impossible as killing the Minotaur.
facts.statement: Ariadne, Minos's daughter, gave Theseus a ball of thread to navigate out of the Labyrinth after killing the Minotaur.
facts.wowFactor: Killing the Minotaur was the hard part — but escaping the Labyrinth was what would have actually killed Theseus. The Labyrinth was literally designed to be inescapable. A ball of thread was the solution. Simple things save heroes.
facts.visualDescription: A Cretan princess secretly handing a golden ball of thread to a young hero at night, the entrance to the Labyrinth looming behind her in shadow

variants.question: What did Theseus do with Ariadne's thread inside the Labyrinth?
variants.correctAnswer: He tied it at the entrance and unrolled it as he walked, then followed it back out
distractors: He used it to strangle the Minotaur
He wove it into a net to trap the Minotaur
He tied the Minotaur with it to drag it out
He left it as an offering to the gods

variants.question: How did Ariadne know how to solve the Labyrinth — what was her source for the thread trick?
variants.correctAnswer: Daedalus, who designed the Labyrinth, told her the secret
distractors: She had memorized every turn from the blueprints she stole
Athena came to her in a dream with the solution
She had bribed a Labyrinth guard for a map
She had entered it herself as a child and remembered the way

variants.question: True or false: Ariadne gave Theseus a ball of thread to find his way out of the Labyrinth.
King Minos's daughter ___ gave Theseus the thread that let him escape the Labyrinth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god found Ariadne weeping after Theseus abandoned her on the island of Naxos, and made her his wife?
facts.explanation: After escaping Crete with Ariadne, Theseus stopped at the island of Naxos. In a much-debated moment in mythology, he sailed away while Ariadne slept — abandoning the woman who had saved his life. Ancient sources disagree on why (divine command, fickle heart, or necessity). Dionysus, the god of wine and ecstasy, found the weeping Ariadne, fell in love with her, married her, and eventually made her immortal. Ariadne's story ends better than Theseus's.
facts.statement: Theseus abandoned Ariadne on Naxos; Dionysus found her there and married her, eventually making her immortal.
facts.wowFactor: Theseus abandoned the woman who saved his life, his entire quest, and Athens's freedom — and she was found by a god who actually appreciated her and made her immortal. Ariadne ended up winning.
facts.visualDescription: A woman weeping alone on a rocky island shore at dawn, a divine figure wreathed in grapevines and golden light approaching from the sea

variants.question: On which island did Theseus abandon Ariadne?
What ultimately happened to Ariadne after Dionysus found and married her?
distractors: She was transformed into a constellation
She was given rulership of Naxos
She returned to Crete and became its queen
She became the priestess of Dionysus's temple

variants.question: True or false: After Theseus abandoned Ariadne, Dionysus found her and eventually made her his wife.
The god ___ found the abandoned Ariadne on Naxos and married her.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Athenian king, watching for his son Theseus's return, jumped into the sea when he saw the black sails still raised — dying before learning of Theseus's victory?
facts.explanation: Before leaving for Crete, Theseus promised his father Aegeus that if he succeeded, he would raise white sails on the return voyage — black sails would mean he had died. Theseus, in his excitement after killing the Minotaur, forgot to change the sails. Aegeus, watching from a cliff (Cape Sounion), saw black sails approaching and, believing his son was dead, threw himself into the sea and drowned. The body of water was named the Aegean Sea in his honor. Theseus arrived to find his father dead and himself king.
facts.statement: Theseus forgot to change from black to white sails; his father Aegeus saw the black sails and jumped to his death — giving the Aegean Sea its name.
facts.wowFactor: The hero who saved Athens forgot to change his sails. His triumphant homecoming became his greatest tragedy. His father died steps away from rescue, and Theseus became king of a city that had just lost its king because he forgot to swap a flag.
facts.visualDescription: An old king standing on a sea cliff watching black-sailed ships approaching, his face contorted in grief, the great blue sea spreading below him bearing his name forever

variants.question: What had Theseus promised his father before sailing to Crete?
variants.correctAnswer: To raise white sails if he survived — black sails meant death
distractors: To sacrifice the Minotaur's horn at Poseidon's temple on return
To bring back Ariadne as his bride
To sail directly to Athens without stopping
To return within thirty days or count himself lost

variants.question: What sea was named after Theseus's father Aegeus, who drowned there?
True or false: King Aegeus drowned himself because he saw black sails and believed Theseus had been killed.
Theseus forgot to change the sails from black to white, and his father ___ jumped to his death seeing them.

facts.quizQuestion: Jason assembled history's greatest band of hero adventurers to sail with him. What were they called?
facts.explanation: Jason recruited the greatest heroes of his age — including Heracles, Orpheus, Castor and Pollux, and Atalanta — to crew his ship the Argo and sail to Colchis in search of the Golden Fleece. The Argonauts are mythology's ultimate all-star team.
facts.statement: Jason assembled the Argonauts, the greatest band of heroes ever gathered, to sail in search of the Golden Fleece.
facts.wowFactor: The Argonauts were mythology's ultimate superhero team — Heracles, Orpheus, Atalanta, and dozens more crammed onto one ship. It's the original Avengers.
facts.visualDescription: Massive wooden ship crowded with muscular heroes in gleaming armor, oars churning the sea, a massive golden ram's fleece banner at the prow

variants.question: The Argonauts were assembled by which Greek hero for a quest to find the Golden Fleece?
Jason's ship was named the Argo — what were his crew of heroes called?
True or false: Jason assembled a group of heroes called the Argonauts to sail in search of the Golden Fleece.
Jason and the ___ sailed to Colchis aboard the ship Argo to find the Golden Fleece.

facts.quizQuestion: Jason sailed the Argo to which distant kingdom at the edge of the world to claim the Golden Fleece?
facts.explanation: The Golden Fleece was kept in Colchis, a kingdom on the eastern shore of the Black Sea (modern Georgia). King Aeetes possessed it and had no intention of giving it up — he set Jason impossible tasks, never expecting him to survive. Colchis was so far east that Greeks considered it the edge of the known world.
facts.statement: The Golden Fleece was kept in Colchis, a distant kingdom at the edge of the known world.
facts.wowFactor: Colchis was real — it's modern-day Georgia on the Black Sea. Ancient Greeks may have been inspired by actual gold-washing practices there, where fleeces were used to pan for gold dust in rivers.
facts.visualDescription: Ancient ship sailing through stormy seas toward a rocky distant shore where a golden fleece glimmers from a tree in a dark sacred grove

variants.question: Colchis was the destination of Jason's quest — what treasure was kept there?
Jason's voyage took him to the far eastern edge of the known world. What was the name of that kingdom?
True or false: The Golden Fleece was kept in Troy, and Jason had to steal it during the Trojan War.
Jason sailed to ___, a kingdom at the edge of the world, to claim the Golden Fleece.

facts.quizQuestion: Which sorceress princess helped Jason yoke fire-breathing bulls, defeat the Spartoi warriors, and drug the sleepless dragon guarding the Fleece?
facts.explanation: Medea was the daughter of King Aeetes of Colchis and a granddaughter of Helios, the sun god. She was a priestess of the goddess Hecate and her sorcery was formidable. When she saw Jason, Aphrodite (at Eros's prompting) made her fall devastatingly in love with him. She gave him an ointment made from the Caucasian crocus — a plant that grew where Prometheus's blood fell during his eagle-punishment — that made him invulnerable to fire and iron for a single day. She also gave him the crucial tactical insight about the Spartoi: throw a stone among them and they'll fight each other. Without these three contributions Jason would have died in the first trial. Her story continues in myth_medea_revenge later in this sub-deck, where the same capabilities that saved him are turned against him.
facts.statement: Medea gave Jason fireproof skin, told him how to defeat an army of skeleton warriors, and put the guardian dragon to sleep — she was the real protagonist of the Fleece quest.
facts.wowFactor: Every impossible trial King Aeetes designed — fireproof bulls, skeleton army, sleepless dragon — was solved not by Jason's skill but by his enemy's daughter. He was essentially a delivery mechanism for Medea's power. Greek mythology celebrated him as the hero. His name is the one we remember.
facts.visualDescription: Colchian temple at midnight, dark-haired sorceress grinding luminous purple petals in a stone bowl, firelight catching her eyes while she works, outside the window a massive coiled shape visible in the sacred grove, and standing in the doorway — looking at her with careful gratitude — a golden-haired man in borrowed armor

variants.question: Medea helped Jason survive three deadly trials in Colchis. Who set those trials?
Jason had to yoke fire-breathing bulls, fight skeleton warriors, and drug a dragon — who gave him the magic to survive?
True or false: Jason defeated the dragon guarding the Golden Fleece by fighting it in single combat.
The sorceress ___ used her magic to help Jason survive the trials of King Aeetes and steal the Golden Fleece.

facts.quizQuestion: As one of King Aeetes' trials, Jason had to sow a field with these — which instantly sprouted into a fully armed army of warriors.
facts.explanation: King Aeetes gave Jason dragon's teeth to sow in a plowed field. The moment they hit the soil, they grew into the Spartoi — a fully armed army of skeleton warriors. Medea told Jason to throw a rock into their midst; the warriors, confused about who threw it, turned on each other and destroyed themselves.
facts.statement: Jason sowed dragon's teeth that instantly sprouted into a fully armed army of skeleton warriors called the Spartoi.
facts.wowFactor: The dragon's teeth that Jason sowed were actually the same teeth from the dragon slain by Cadmus, founder of Thebes. Greek mythology has shared props — the same cursed teeth that built one city were used to nearly destroy another hero.
facts.visualDescription: Warrior in bronze armor hurling a jagged stone into a writhing mass of skeleton soldiers erupting from dark soil, weapons clashing in chaos

variants.question: The Spartoi were skeleton warriors that grew from the ground — what was sown to create them?
King Aeetes ordered Jason to plow a field and sow it — what did he have to plant that would spring up as armed warriors?
True or false: Jason defeated the Spartoi skeleton warriors by fighting them all individually in hand-to-hand combat.
Jason planted ___ in a field, which instantly sprouted into an army of armed skeleton warriors he had to defeat.

facts.quizQuestion: Jason abandoned this woman — who had sacrificed everything to help him — for a Greek princess. She killed their children in revenge.
facts.explanation: After the quest, Medea and Jason lived in Corinth. When Jason decided to marry the princess Glauce for political advancement, Medea's revenge was devastating: she sent Glauce a poisoned robe that burned her to death, killed King Creon, and murdered her own children by Jason before fleeing on her dragon-drawn chariot. It is one of antiquity's most horrifying betrayal stories.
facts.statement: Jason betrayed Medea for a political marriage, and she killed their children in the most devastating act of revenge in Greek mythology.
facts.wowFactor: Medea's revenge is so extreme it defined the word 'infanticide' in ancient Greek culture — and audiences still feel the horror. She did it knowing it would destroy her too, just so Jason would suffer every remaining day of his life.
facts.visualDescription: Anguished woman in a dragon-drawn chariot rising into the sky, arms reaching back toward two children collapsed below, a burning palace in the distance

variants.question: When Jason abandoned her for a Greek princess, which sorceress killed their children in revenge?
Jason had political ambitions and left his wife for a Corinthian princess — whose revenge became one of the most horrifying in all mythology?
True or false: After abandoning Medea, Jason lived happily ever after with his new princess bride.
When Jason left ___ for a new bride, she killed their children and fled on a dragon-drawn chariot in revenge.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero was the greatest musician who ever lived — his lyre music could charm wild animals, make trees uproot and follow him, and move stones?
facts.explanation: Orpheus was the son of Apollo, god of music and prophecy, and Calliope, chief of the nine Muses and patron of epic poetry. The talent he inherited was not metaphorically great — in the myths it was literally cosmic. Rivers physically diverted course to listen. Trees physically moved. Stones gathered closer. This is the same Orpheus who in life would silence Tartarus's punishments with the same lyre (myth_orpheus_hades_deal, myth_orpheus_hades_weep), and the same Orpheus who aboard the Argo would outplay the Sirens (myth_sirens_orpheus, already in the Monsters sub-deck). His birth from the union of song's father and poetry's mother made him mythology's argument that art, at its highest, is not merely beautiful — it is physical force.
facts.statement: Orpheus played music so powerful that trees uprooted themselves to follow him, rivers paused their flow, and wild beasts lay down peacefully beside their prey.
facts.wowFactor: Ancient Greeks were not speaking in metaphor when they said trees followed Orpheus — the myths present it as literal fact. His music rewrote the behavior of matter. The man who would later move a god who had never shown mercy did that first: he moved the inanimate world. Starting small.
facts.visualDescription: Forest hillside at golden hour, a young man with a lyre sitting on a rock, and around him the impossible: wolves and deer pressed together without aggression, a river making a slow curve toward him against its own gradient, several oaks leaning visibly toward the sound, a hawk on a nearby branch with its head tilted, listening

variants.question: Orpheus could charm animals, trees, and rocks with his playing — what instrument did he play?
Which hero's musical skill was so extraordinary that wild animals became tame and trees walked toward him?
True or false: Orpheus was the greatest musician who ever lived, able to charm animals and even move trees with his music.
___ was the greatest musician who ever lived — his lyre could charm animals, uproot trees, and move stones.

facts.quizQuestion: Orpheus played his lyre for the ruler of the Underworld, who wept tears of iron and agreed to release Eurydice — which god relented?
facts.explanation: Hades wept tears described specifically as iron — the hardest and coldest material the Greeks knew, used for weapons — because Orpheus's music made even the hardest heart feel what it had never felt. Hades had never granted a single mercy in all of time. He granted this one. He added the condition not to be cruel but because the law of death required some acknowledgment: the living world and the dead world had to be kept separate by consent as much as by gates. The condition was simple, humane, and possible — a hundred steps in the dark without looking back. Orpheus failed it. This same Underworld and its king are described in the Underworld sub-deck; the iron tears are the pivot point between Hades the implacable god and Hades the temporarily human one.
facts.statement: Hades agreed to release Eurydice on one condition — Orpheus must not look back before they reach the surface — and wept iron tears as he said it.
facts.wowFactor: Hades had watched every soul that ever existed pass through his kingdom. He had seen every grief, every prayer, every desperate bargain. He felt nothing — until Orpheus played. The one time in all of mythology that death made an exception was also the only time a mortal made death feel grief. The exception still failed.
facts.visualDescription: Vast dark throne room carved from obsidian, the king and his queen seated in absolute stillness, and between them and the musician on the floor a space of charged silence — the king's face not cruel, not kind, but cracked open somehow, one dark tear on a stone cheek, his hand raised in the gesture of grudging assent

variants.question: Hades agreed to release Eurydice on one condition — what was Orpheus forbidden to do on the way out?
Which ruler of the dead was so moved by Orpheus's music that he wept and agreed to free Eurydice?
True or false: Hades agreed to let Eurydice leave the Underworld as long as Orpheus did not look back at her during the journey out.
Orpheus played for ___ in the Underworld, who wept and agreed to release Eurydice on the condition that Orpheus not look back.

facts.quizQuestion: This hero almost rescued his wife from death — but at the last moment, almost at the threshold of the living world, he turned to look at her and lost her forever.
facts.explanation: The versions of the myth differ on why Orpheus turned: doubt that Eurydice was truly following, fear that Hades had deceived him, the simple overwhelming need to see her face after so much darkness. What does not differ is the moment: he turned at the last step. Not in the deep dark of the tunnel — at the threshold where sunlight was already visible. Some versions say she had already been close enough to feel the warmth of the upper world when he turned. Eurydice, still a shade bound by the condition, faded immediately. He reached for her. She was already smoke. The mythological parallel is Hermod's journey to Hel to retrieve Baldr in Norse mythology — another hero who failed on a single condition at the last moment, another resurrection that almost happened.
facts.statement: He made it almost all the way. The threshold of the living world was right there. Then he looked.
facts.wowFactor: The cruelest detail is how far he got. Sisyphus and Tantalus are punished forever and we understand why. Orpheus followed every rule, succeeded at every impossible thing, and failed on the last step — one step — of the easiest thing. Greek tragedy's defining shape is the gap between almost and.
facts.visualDescription: Stone tunnel mouth opening onto a white blaze of morning sunlight, a figure silhouetted just inside, his head turned back at a three-quarter angle — and behind him, where a second silhouette had been, only the faintest suggestion of dissipating smoke, a hand-shape that is already not a hand

variants.question: Orpheus broke Hades' one condition and lost Eurydice forever. What did he do wrong?
Almost at the threshold of the living world, which hero's doubt caused him to glance back — losing his wife to the Underworld forever?
True or false: Orpheus successfully rescued Eurydice from the Underworld and brought her back to the living world.
___ lost Eurydice forever by looking back at her just before they reached the sunlit world — and was later torn apart by the Maenads.

facts.quizQuestion: Bellerophon tamed this famous winged horse with a golden bridle given to him by Athena — which creature was it?
facts.explanation: Bellerophon was tasked with killing the Chimera, a seemingly impossible mission. Athena (or Poseidon, in some versions) provided a golden bridle that gave him power over Pegasus, the divine winged horse born from Medusa's blood. With Pegasus, Bellerophon could attack the Chimera from the air, beyond reach of its fire.
facts.statement: Bellerophon tamed the winged horse Pegasus using a golden bridle from Athena, which he used to kill the Chimera.
facts.wowFactor: Pegasus wasn't born from a mare — he sprang fully formed from the blood of Medusa when Perseus cut off her head. The winged horse is literally the offspring of a Gorgon and Poseidon.
facts.visualDescription: Young warrior with a gleaming golden bridle approaching a magnificent white-winged horse on a rocky mountainside at dawn

variants.question: Pegasus the winged horse was tamed by which hero using a golden bridle from Athena?
Athena gave Bellerophon a golden bridle so he could tame a winged horse — which horse was it?
True or false: Bellerophon tamed Pegasus with a golden bridle gifted by Athena.
Bellerophon used a golden bridle from Athena to tame ___, the winged horse, before riding him to kill the Chimera.

facts.quizQuestion: Bellerophon rode Pegasus to kill this fire-breathing monster — a single creature with the head of a lion, body of a goat, and tail of a serpent.
facts.explanation: The Chimera ravaged the kingdom of Lycia, breathing fire and destroying everything in its path. King Iobates sent Bellerophon to kill it expecting him to die — but riding Pegasus gave Bellerophon the height advantage to stay above the fire, and he killed the Chimera with a lead-tipped spear, the metal melting in the creature's own flames to choke it.
facts.statement: Bellerophon rode Pegasus to kill the Chimera — a fire-breathing monster with the head of a lion, body of a goat, and tail of a serpent.
facts.wowFactor: The word 'chimera' now means any wild impossible idea — because the creature was so absurdly mismatched that Greeks used it as a metaphor for fantasies that can't exist. Bellerophon killed a living metaphor.
facts.visualDescription: Warrior on a white winged horse attacking from above a roaring three-part monster — lion head breathing fire, goat body rearing up, serpent tail striking

variants.question: The Chimera was a fire-breathing lion-goat-serpent monster — which hero rode Pegasus to kill it?
Bellerophon used Pegasus's aerial advantage to kill a famous fire-breather — which monster was it?
True or false: The Chimera was a fire-breathing monster with the body parts of a lion, goat, and serpent.
Bellerophon flew on Pegasus to kill the ___, a fire-breathing monster that was part lion, part goat, and part serpent.

facts.quizQuestion: After killing the Chimera, this hero grew so proud that he tried to fly Pegasus all the way to Mount Olympus — Zeus sent a gadfly to sting the horse, bucking the hero off.
facts.explanation: Drunk on his victories, Bellerophon decided he deserved to live among the gods on Olympus. As he rode Pegasus skyward, Zeus sent a tiny gadfly to sting the horse. Pegasus reared and threw Bellerophon, who fell back to earth. He survived, but was crippled, blinded, and wandered the earth alone and rejected by both gods and mortals — the ultimate punishment for hubris.
facts.statement: Bellerophon tried to fly to Olympus on Pegasus out of hubris — Zeus sent a gadfly that bucked him off, and he wandered crippled for the rest of his life.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest monster-slayer of his age, brought down not by a dragon or a god's wrath, but by a single insect. Zeus didn't even bother fighting him — he just sent a fly.
facts.visualDescription: Hero tumbling from a rearing white winged horse high in the sky, clouds below him, the gleaming peak of Olympus visible but forever out of reach above

variants.question: Zeus sent a gadfly to sting Pegasus and throw off his rider — which hero suffered this punishment for trying to fly to Olympus?
Which hero's pride grew so immense after killing the Chimera that he tried to ride Pegasus all the way to Mount Olympus?
True or false: Bellerophon was punished for his hubris when Zeus sent a gadfly to make Pegasus throw him from the sky.
___ tried to ride Pegasus to Olympus out of pride — Zeus sent a gadfly, the horse threw him, and he wandered the earth crippled and alone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which master craftsman built the Labyrinth for King Minos of Crete to imprison the Minotaur?
facts.explanation: Daedalus was the greatest inventor and craftsman of the ancient world. King Minos commissioned him to build an inescapable maze — the Labyrinth — beneath his palace at Knossos to house the Minotaur, a monster born of his wife Pasiphae. Daedalus made it so complex that even he could barely find his way out.
facts.statement: Daedalus, the greatest inventor of the ancient world, built the Labyrinth for King Minos to imprison the Minotaur.
facts.wowFactor: The Labyrinth was so perfectly designed that even its architect could barely navigate it. The real Palace of Knossos in Crete has so many rooms and corridors that archaeologists believe it inspired the myth.
facts.visualDescription: Bearded craftsman in a workshop sketching intricate maze blueprints, stone tools and plans spread around him, with a glimpse of dark tunnels being carved below

variants.question: The Labyrinth was built to imprison the Minotaur — who designed and constructed it for King Minos?
King Minos of Crete needed an inescapable prison for the Minotaur — which craftsman built the Labyrinth for him?
True or false: Daedalus built the Labyrinth for King Minos to imprison the Minotaur.
___ built the Labyrinth — an inescapable maze beneath King Minos's palace — to hold the Minotaur captive.

facts.quizQuestion: After building the Labyrinth, Daedalus and his son Icarus were imprisoned in it by which king — who feared Daedalus would reveal its secrets?
facts.explanation: King Minos imprisoned Daedalus and Icarus in the very Labyrinth Daedalus had built, to prevent him from helping anyone else navigate or escape it. Daedalus had already helped Ariadne (by telling her about the thread trick), which had allowed Theseus to kill the Minotaur. Minos controlled all the ships around Crete, so escape by sea was impossible.
facts.statement: King Minos imprisoned Daedalus and Icarus in the Labyrinth, fearing Daedalus would share its secrets with enemies.
facts.wowFactor: Daedalus was trapped in his own greatest creation — a prison so perfect it could hold its own architect. There's a grim poetic justice to being caught in your own masterpiece.
facts.visualDescription: Father and son in torchlit stone corridors of a vast maze, one looking upward toward a sliver of light above, the other sketching feathers on the dungeon floor

variants.question: Daedalus and Icarus were imprisoned in the Labyrinth — who imprisoned them there?
Why did King Minos imprison Daedalus — the very man who built the Labyrinth?
variants.correctAnswer: To prevent him from revealing its secrets
distractors: Because Daedalus stole gold from the palace
Because Daedalus helped Theseus escape Troy
Because Daedalus claimed to be smarter than Zeus

variants.question: True or false: King Minos imprisoned Daedalus and Icarus in the Labyrinth that Daedalus himself had built.
___ imprisoned Daedalus and Icarus in the Labyrinth to prevent Daedalus from sharing its secrets.

facts.quizQuestion: Which young man escaped the Labyrinth on wings of wax and feathers — but flew too close to the sun, melting his wings, and fell into the sea?
facts.explanation: Daedalus crafted wings from feathers and beeswax for himself and his son Icarus. He warned Icarus not to fly too low (the sea spray would weigh down the feathers) or too high (the sun would melt the wax). Icarus, exhilarated by flight, climbed higher and higher until the sun melted the wax. His feathers scattered and he plunged into the sea, which was afterward called the Icarian Sea.
facts.statement: Icarus ignored his father's warning, flew too close to the sun, melted his wax wings, and fell into the sea.
facts.wowFactor: Daedalus's warning was perfect and specific — not too high, not too low. Icarus knew exactly what would happen. He flew high anyway. His fall is the oldest metaphor for ignoring good advice.
facts.visualDescription: Young man tumbling from the sky, feathers scattering around him as wax drips from dissolving wings, the blazing sun above and dark sea waiting below

variants.question: Icarus fell into the sea after his wings melted — who built those wings and warned him not to fly too high?
Which son of Daedalus ignored his father's warning and flew too close to the sun, causing his wax wings to melt?
True or false: Icarus fell into the sea because he flew too close to the sun and his wax wings melted.
___ flew too close to the sun on wings of wax and feathers, melting them, and fell into the sea.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero was abandoned at birth, raised by bears in the wilderness, and grew up to become the fastest mortal who ever lived?
facts.explanation: Atalanta's father wanted a son and abandoned her on a hillside. A she-bear nursed and raised her, and she grew up as a wild huntress, eventually recognized as the fastest mortal alive. She was one of the few women to join the Argonauts and was among the hunters of the Calydonian Boar.
facts.statement: Atalanta was abandoned at birth, raised by bears, and became the fastest mortal alive — a huntress who bested nearly every man she met.
facts.wowFactor: Raised by bears, faster than any man, slayer of the Calydonian Boar — Atalanta is arguably the most badass mortal in all Greek mythology, and almost nobody talks about her.
facts.visualDescription: Athletic young woman in huntress garb sprinting barefoot through a forest, bow across her back, with bears visible watching from the tree line behind her

variants.question: Atalanta was raised by bears and became the fastest mortal alive — what happened to her at birth?
variants.correctAnswer: She was abandoned on a hillside
distractors: She was hidden in a temple
She was raised in the wilderness by Artemis herself
She fell from the sky as an infant

variants.question: Which Greek hero was abandoned at birth, raised by wild animals, and became the fastest mortal who ever lived?
True or false: Atalanta was abandoned at birth, raised by bears, and grew up to be the fastest mortal in Greece.
___ was abandoned at birth, raised by bears, and became the fastest mortal who ever lived.

facts.quizQuestion: Atalanta vowed to marry only a man who could beat her in a footrace. Hippomenes won by dropping these along the track to distract her — a gift from Aphrodite.
facts.explanation: Atalanta declared that any suitor must beat her in a race or be killed. Hippomenes asked Aphrodite for help; she gave him three golden apples from the Garden of the Hesperides. During the race, each time Atalanta pulled ahead, Hippomenes rolled a golden apple off to the side. Atalanta, unable to resist the gleaming treasure, veered to pick each one up — and Hippomenes won.
facts.statement: Hippomenes won his race against Atalanta by dropping three golden apples (from Aphrodite) along the track to distract her.
facts.wowFactor: The fastest mortal alive, unbeatable in a race, brought down by shiny objects. Aphrodite understood something about human nature: even heroes are distracted by beautiful things.
facts.visualDescription: Sprinting woman veering from her path to snatch a glowing golden apple that has rolled into the grass, while a male runner surges ahead on the track

variants.question: Hippomenes used three golden apples to beat Atalanta in a footrace — which goddess provided the apples?
Atalanta had beaten every suitor in a footrace — what did Hippomenes drop along the track to distract her long enough to win?
True or false: Hippomenes beat Atalanta in a footrace by dropping golden apples given to him by Aphrodite to distract her.
Hippomenes won his race against Atalanta by dropping ___ — a gift from Aphrodite — to distract her along the track.

facts.quizQuestion: The second greatest Greek warrior at Troy — after Achilles — who went mad and killed himself after losing the armor of Achilles to Odysseus in a debate contest?
