Psyche bypassed Cerberus by feeding him a ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which fire-breathing monster had a lion's head, a goat's body, and a serpent's tail?
facts.explanation: The Chimera was the offspring of Typhon and Echidna, placing it in the same monster family as Cerberus, the Hydra, and the Nemean Lion — a fact that makes Bellerophon's hunt part of the same larger project of clearing Echidna's children from the world. Ancient sources add a goat's head growing from its back as a second head, making it even stranger than the three-part description usually cited. It ravaged Lycia until King Iobates, wanting Bellerophon dead without the political risk of executing him directly, sent him against it — expecting the Chimera to do his work for him.
facts.statement: The Chimera was a fire-breathing monster with a lion's head, a goat's body, and a serpent for a tail — and its name now means any impossible hybrid in English.
facts.wowFactor: The word 'chimera' entered science to describe organisms with two genetically distinct cell populations — and philosophy to mean any impossible dream. A single three-species monster gave the world a technical term used in genetics laboratories and a metaphor for unattainable ambition. Ancient Greeks were naming things we still need words for.
facts.visualDescription: Three-natured beast on a burning mountainside at dusk: the lion's head forward and roaring orange flame, a goat's head craning backward from the spine with yellow eyes, the serpent tail rearing above them both, scales gleaming copper in the firelight, the village below already ash

variants.question: The Chimera breathed fire and had three animal parts — what was its tail?
Where did the Chimera live and terrorize, prompting the king to send Bellerophon?
True or false: The Chimera had three animal components — lion, goat, and serpent.
The fire-breathing monster with a lion's head, goat's body, and serpent's tail was the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero killed the Chimera by riding Pegasus and pouring molten lead down its throat?
facts.explanation: Pegasus — born from Medusa's blood in the Monsters sub-deck — becomes Bellerophon's defining tool here: aerial superiority made the Chimera's fire-breath useless. Bellerophon attached lead to his spear tip; when the Chimera breathed fire at his aerial attacks, the lead melted in the heat of its own throat and poured down, killing it from within. It is arguably the most inventive kill in Greek monster-fighting — using the monster's own ability as the mechanism of its death. The victory threads directly back to Athena's golden bridle gift and forward to Bellerophon's hubris: he then tried to fly Pegasus to Olympus itself, and Zeus sent a gadfly to sting the horse, throwing Bellerophon to the earth to wander blind and lame for the rest of his life.
facts.statement: Bellerophon killed the Chimera by riding Pegasus above its range and using the monster's own fire to melt a lead-tipped spear down its throat.
facts.wowFactor: Bellerophon used the Chimera's fire-breath as the delivery mechanism for its own death. The monster burned itself from the inside. He is then broken by Zeus for the hubris of attempting Olympus — the same gods whose tools gave him the victory punished him for enjoying it too much. Victory in Greek mythology almost always has this shape.
facts.visualDescription: White-winged horse banking high above a mountain, rider leaning forward with a spear angled downward, the lead tip beginning to glow red as a column of orange flame erupts toward it from below, the three-part monster visible tiny and furious on the slope beneath them

variants.question: Bellerophon killed the Chimera using Pegasus for aerial advantage — what was his weapon?
variants.correctAnswer: A lead-tipped spear melted by the Chimera's own fire

variants.question: How did Bellerophon obtain Pegasus for his quest against the Chimera?
variants.correctAnswer: With a golden bridle given by Athena
distractors: He captured it after killing Medusa
He inherited it from his father Poseidon
He stole it from the Muses on Mount Helicon

variants.question: True or false: Bellerophon killed the Chimera by riding Pegasus and pouring molten lead down its throat.
The hero who killed the Chimera while riding Pegasus was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: The Sphinx's riddle asked: 'What walks on four legs in the morning, two at noon, and three in the evening?' — what is the answer?
facts.explanation: The Sphinx had been stationed outside Thebes — sent by Hera as a punishment, or by the Muses for reasons lost to history — and was killing every traveler who failed the riddle. Oedipus arrived fleeing his own fate (the Oracle had told him he would kill his father and marry his mother), having already unknowingly killed his father Laius at a crossroads. He answered correctly, the Sphinx threw herself from her cliff, and Thebes rewarded him with the throne and the widowed queen — his own mother. The riddle's answer was man, but the tragedy is that Oedipus, who understood humanity's arc from crawling to staff, never understood himself. The Sphinx's story is inseparable from the Oedipus facts in the Love, Tragedy & Fate sub-deck.
facts.statement: The Sphinx's riddle was 'What walks on four legs in the morning, two at noon, and three in the evening?' — and when Oedipus answered 'man,' she destroyed herself.
facts.wowFactor: The riddle's answer is 'man' — and the man answering it was in the process of fulfilling a prophecy about the worst things a human being can do. He solved the riddle of the human lifespan while living out the most extreme violation of human bonds. The Sphinx chose the right questioner.
facts.visualDescription: Winged lion-woman on a high pale cliff, one taloned forepaw raised in arrested threat, facing a lone figure below her on the mountain road who stands relaxed, arms at sides, answering — while on the slope around him, barely visible in the tall grass, are the pale remains of travelers who answered wrong

variants.question: The answer to the Sphinx's riddle is 'man' — what represented the 'three legs in the evening'?
variants.correctAnswer: A walking stick used in old age
distractors: The support of two friends in battle

variants.question: What happened to the Sphinx when Oedipus correctly answered her riddle?
distractors: She transformed into a stone statue
She was imprisoned by the gods

variants.question: True or false: The Sphinx devoured all travelers who failed to answer her riddle correctly.
The answer to the Sphinx's riddle — what walks on four legs, then two, then three — is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: When the Cyclops Polyphemus asked Odysseus his name before being blinded, what name did Odysseus give?
facts.explanation: Trapped in Polyphemus's cave with his men being eaten two at a time, Odysseus devised a multi-stage plan: get the Cyclops drunk on wine gifted by the priest of Apollo, reveal the false name, blind him with a heated stake while he slept, then escape clinging to the undersides of his sheep so Polyphemus's searching hands could not find them. The name trick worked perfectly — the other Cyclopes, hearing 'Nobody is hurting me!', concluded Polyphemus was suffering divine madness and left. Odysseus then ruined the plan completely by shouting his real name from the departing ship, allowing Polyphemus to pray a specific curse to his father Poseidon — launching the decade of wandering that structures The Odyssey sub-deck.
facts.statement: Odysseus told the Cyclops his name was 'Nobody' — so when the other Cyclopes came running at his screams, Polyphemus could only shout 'Nobody is hurting me.'
facts.wowFactor: The plan was perfect until the hero's ego broke it. Odysseus outthought a giant through linguistics and escaped against every odd — then bellowed his real name from the ship because he needed Polyphemus to know who had beaten him. Poseidon heard. The whole Odyssey is the price of that boast.
facts.visualDescription: Inside a dark cave, six men pressed flat against the wall above the entrance, each gripping the thick wool of a massive sheep from below, while a colossal one-eyed figure with a smoking socket crouches at the entrance running his hands along every sheep's back, fingers probing, finding nothing

variants.question: Odysseus used the 'Nobody' trick on the Cyclops — how did he actually blind Polyphemus?
variants.correctAnswer: With a sharpened wooden stake heated in fire
distractors: With a bronze spear from Athena
By throwing hot coals in his eye

variants.question: Why did Odysseus's 'Nobody' trick backfire in the long run?
variants.correctAnswer: He boasted his real name as he escaped, letting Poseidon know who blinded his son
distractors: The other Cyclopes eventually realized the trick
His crew revealed his name while rowing away
Athena forced him to confess the truth

variants.question: True or false: Odysseus told the Cyclops his name was 'Nobody' to prevent the other Cyclopes from helping after he was blinded.
To trick the Cyclops Polyphemus, Odysseus said his name was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Polyphemus the Cyclops was the son of which god, whose curse drove Odysseus's years of wandering?
facts.explanation: The cruel arithmetic of the Odyssey: Odysseus had Athena as his patron, but Poseidon controlled every sea he needed to cross. Blinding Polyphemus was the act that activated this opposition — a god's grief and fury channeled against one man's route home. Poseidon could not kill Odysseus outright (Fate prevented it) but could destroy his ships, drown his crew, and drive him to every far shore in the Mediterranean. The ten years of the Odyssey (mirroring the ten years of the Trojan War in the Trojan War sub-deck) are Poseidon making the shortest sea voyage take a decade.
facts.statement: Polyphemus was Poseidon's son — blinding him gave Odysseus Athena's protection on one side and Poseidon's hatred on the other, for ten years.
facts.wowFactor: Odysseus crossed the same sea every Ionian sailor crossed routinely. Poseidon made it take a decade. He sank ships, called storms, and drove the hero to islands at the world's edge — all because his son, who had been eating people alive, lost an eye. Divine proportionality was never Greek mythology's strong suit.
facts.visualDescription: Cyclops standing waist-deep in the Aegean at sunset, one ruined eye socket, both arms raised toward the sky in prayer, his massive form dark against the orange water, a tiny ship visible far below the horizon already pulling its oars hard for open sea

variants.question: Poseidon cursed Odysseus because Odysseus had blinded which of his sons?
What specifically did Polyphemus pray to Poseidon when asking for revenge on Odysseus?
variants.correctAnswer: That Odysseus never reach home, or if he must, arrive late and miserable with all companions dead
distractors: That Odysseus be turned into a pig like Circe's victims
That Odysseus be imprisoned on Calypso's island forever
That every Greek sailor be punished for Odysseus's crime

variants.question: True or false: The Cyclops Polyphemus was the son of Poseidon, whose curse plagued Odysseus's journey home.
The Cyclops Polyphemus was the son of the god ___.

facts.quizQuestion: To hear the Sirens' song safely, Odysseus ordered his crew to tie him to what part of the ship?
facts.explanation: Circe warned Odysseus about the Sirens: creatures part-woman, part-bird whose singing drew sailors onto the rocks. His solution was engineered around a known weakness in himself — he knew he would try to go to them if he heard the song, so he removed his own ability to act on that impulse. The crew's ears were filled with beeswax; Odysseus was lashed to the mast with orders that no matter what he said, screamed, or commanded, no one was to release him until they were past. He heard everything. He begged and screamed to be untied. His men, following his earlier orders, bound him tighter. This same tactical problem — how do you act on information you know your future self will reject — is now called the Ulysses contract in behavioral economics.
facts.statement: Odysseus was the only mortal in mythology to hear the Sirens' full song and survive — not through strength or divine protection, but through pre-committed restraint.
facts.wowFactor: Odysseus invented behavioral economics. He knew he would make a catastrophic decision under the influence of something irresistible, so he pre-committed himself before the influence arrived. The Ulysses contract — removing your future self's ability to betray your present self's better judgment — is a real concept in finance, addiction medicine, and decision theory. One boat trip created a field of study.
facts.visualDescription: Hero at the mast, ropes wound five times around his arms and chest, back arched, face turned toward jagged rocks where pale singing figures sit motionless — his crew at the oars cannot see the figures and will not look, beeswax visible in their ears, pulling steadily, the ship already past the worst of it

variants.question: Odysseus tied himself to the mast to hear the Sirens — what did he use to protect his crew from the song?
distractors: He ordered them to wear helmets
He had them look away from the Sirens
He asked Athena to deafen them temporarily
He gave them a sleeping potion

variants.question: Who warned Odysseus about the Sirens and advised his method of surviving their song?
True or false: Odysseus was tied to the mast so he could hear the Sirens' song without being able to follow it.
To safely hear the Sirens, Odysseus was tied to the ship's ___.

facts.quizQuestion: When the Argonauts passed the Sirens, which hero drowned out their song by playing his lyre louder?
facts.explanation: The Argonauts' passage past the Sirens is a direct mirror of Odysseus's in The Odyssey sub-deck — but where Odysseus used beeswax and restraint (an engineering solution), Orpheus used surpassing art (an aesthetic one). His lyre did not block the Sirens' song but replaced it. The crew heard something more beautiful than the thing that would have killed them and rowed past. One Argonaut — Butes — still jumped overboard, overcome; Aphrodite saved him from drowning. According to some versions, the Sirens were fated to die if any ship passed them safely — after Odysseus, after the Argo, they fulfilled that fate. Two ships in a row broke their power: one through the refusal to listen, one through listening to something better.
facts.statement: When the Argonauts sailed past the Sirens, Orpheus outplayed them — drowning their fatal song with something more beautiful, and saving the crew.
facts.wowFactor: The Sirens were undefeated for as long as mythology records — no ship, no crew, no god had ever passed them safely. Odysseus tied himself to a mast to survive hearing them. Orpheus just played louder. Where self-control was one man's solution, beauty was another's. Both worked exactly once.
facts.visualDescription: Ship under full sail in open sea, a musician standing at the prow playing upright and fierce, his instrument pouring light across the water, and on the rocks to starboard three pale figures with bird-lower-bodies and open mouths — singing, but their sound bent away from the ship like wind around a stone

variants.question: Orpheus saved the Argonauts from the Sirens by out-playing them — what was his instrument?
Which Argonaut still jumped overboard despite Orpheus's music, and was saved by a goddess?
True or false: Orpheus defeated the Sirens by playing his lyre more beautifully than they sang.
The Argonaut who out-played the Sirens with his lyre was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess gave Bellerophon a golden bridle to tame Pegasus?
facts.explanation: Pegasus was born from Medusa's blood (Monsters sub-deck) and flew to Mount Helicon, where a hoof-strike created the Muses' sacred spring Hippocrene. When Bellerophon needed to ride him, no mortal skill was enough — only a divine bridle could tame a divine horse. The seer Polyidus sent him to sleep in Athena's temple, and she provided the solution overnight. The Chimera (also in this sub-deck) was only killable from the air, making Pegasus essential to the kill — Athena gave Bellerophon the precise tool the task required, then watched him eventually attempt to fly to Olympus on the same horse, and arranged his fall through Zeus.
facts.statement: Athena appeared to Bellerophon in a dream and left a golden bridle beside him — which he used the next morning to tame Pegasus at the spring Pirene.
facts.wowFactor: Bellerophon slew the Chimera, the Amazons, and the Solymi in successive impossible tasks, each time surviving against every expectation. He then tried to ride Pegasus to Olympus itself — and Zeus sent a gadfly to sting the horse mid-flight. Bellerophon fell back to earth and spent the rest of his life lame, blind, and alone, wandering the fields he had once soared above.
facts.visualDescription: Moonlit meadow, a winged white horse drinking from a still spring pool, a man stepping forward slowly with a gleaming golden bridle extended in both hands, the horse's head rising to look at him without fear, great wings half-folded, the reflection of both showing in the dark water below them

variants.question: Athena gave Bellerophon a golden bridle — what did he use it to tame?
How did Bellerophon ultimately meet his downfall after years of heroic success?
variants.correctAnswer: He tried to ride Pegasus to Olympus and was thrown by a Zeus-sent gadfly
distractors: He was killed by the Chimera's brother
He offended Poseidon by refusing to sacrifice Pegasus
He was turned into stone by Medusa's sisters
He drowned trying to cross the sea to Troy

variants.question: True or false: Bellerophon tamed Pegasus using a golden bridle given to him by Athena.
The goddess who gave Bellerophon a golden bridle to tame Pegasus was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which monstrous giant — the most fearsome creature ever born — challenged Zeus for rulership of the cosmos?
facts.explanation: Typhon was Gaia's last weapon against the Olympians, created after the defeat of the Titans as a final attempt to overthrow Zeus's order. He was enormous in every mythological account — heads touching the stars, arms spanning continents, a hundred serpent heads roaring different animal sounds simultaneously. In the most dramatic version of the battle, Typhon overpowered Zeus, cut the tendons from his hands and feet so he could not fight, and stored them in a cave guarded by the she-dragon Delphyne. Only Hermes's cunning and Pan's panic-inducing shriek allowed the tendons to be recovered and Zeus to fight the rematch. He won by burying Typhon under Mount Etna — whose eruptions are Typhon's ongoing rage.
facts.statement: Typhon almost won — he defeated Zeus, cut out his tendons, and left the king of the gods helpless in a cave before Hermes and Pan rescued him.
facts.wowFactor: Zeus, king of gods, wielder of lightning, was temporarily a helpless prisoner — de-tendoned and hidden in a cave by a monster. The king of the cosmos lay immobilized while two trickster gods recovered his muscle tissue from a snake-guarded jar. This is the same Hermes who guides souls to the Underworld; here he's performing a divine surgery heist.
facts.visualDescription: A storm of impossible scale: hundred serpent heads against a sky of solid lightning, each head a different screaming face, and beneath them a figure on the ground reaching upward — not yet defeated, throwing a bolt that meets the chaos above, a volcano already beginning to crack open in the earth behind the monster

variants.question: Typhon was buried under Mount Etna by Zeus — what natural phenomenon does this explain?
distractors: Tsunamis on the coast of Sicily

variants.question: What did Typhon do to Zeus during their initial battle that nearly cost him the cosmos?
variants.correctAnswer: Cut out and stole Zeus's tendons so he couldn't move
distractors: Stole Zeus's thunderbolts and hid them in the sea
Bound Zeus to a rock with divine chains

variants.question: True or false: Typhon was the most fearsome monster in Greek mythology, the last great challenge to Zeus's rule.
The monstrous giant buried under Mount Etna after challenging Zeus was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Typhon fathered many monsters with which half-woman, half-serpent creature known as the 'Mother of Monsters'?
facts.explanation: Echidna — half beautiful woman from the waist up, half massive serpent below — was immortal and lived in a cave untouched by age. She and Typhon produced what amounts to the entire gallery of opponents in the Monsters & Creatures sub-deck: Cerberus guards the Underworld, the Hydra poisons Heracles's legacy, the Chimera kills travelers in Lycia, the Sphinx kills travelers outside Thebes, and the Nemean Lion begins the Twelve Labors. Heracles's career was substantially an assault on Echidna's children — she was eventually killed in her sleep by the hundred-eyed Argus, her immortality finally ending through ambush rather than combat.
facts.statement: Typhon and Echidna were the founding couple of Greek monsters — between them they produced Cerberus, the Hydra, the Chimera, the Sphinx, the Nemean Lion, and more.
facts.wowFactor: Heracles did not just fight monsters — he systematically dismantled a single family. Every Echidna child he killed reduced the population of the most dangerous lineage in Greek mythology. When he descended into the Underworld to capture Cerberus, it was his last fight with his old enemy's last living guardian. He returned it afterward. Even Heracles respected the lock on death's door.
facts.visualDescription: Underground cave lit by bioluminescent stone: a woman with a face of grave, unearthly beauty seated on a ledge, the lower half of her body a coiled serpent that fills the cavern floor, and pressed against the walls behind her in the shadows — glimpses of fur, scale, wing, and horn, each shape suggesting a different future nightmare

variants.question: Echidna was the 'Mother of Monsters' — which of her offspring guarded the Underworld?
How did Echidna's immortal life eventually end, according to myth?
variants.correctAnswer: She was killed in her sleep by the hundred-eyed giant Argus
distractors: She was destroyed when Typhon was buried under Etna
Heracles killed her after the Twelve Labors
She was turned to stone by Medusa's head
Zeus struck her with a thunderbolt as punishment

variants.question: True or false: Echidna was known as the 'Mother of Monsters' and was half woman, half serpent.
The 'Mother of Monsters' who bore Cerberus, Hydra, and Chimera with Typhon was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Most centaurs were savage and wild, but which wise centaur tutored heroes like Achilles, Jason, and Heracles?
facts.explanation: Centaurs in Greek mythology were bywords for violence and drunkenness, creatures who could not be trusted at banquets. Chiron was their antithesis: a teacher of medicine, music, archery, and ethics who tutored Achilles (Trojan War sub-deck), Jason (Heroes & Quests sub-deck), and Heracles. His death is mythology's most quietly devastating: a Hydra-poisoned arrow from Heracles — shot during a centaur brawl, aimed elsewhere — grazed him. Immortal, he could not die from the wound. Mortal, he could not heal from the Hydra's venom. He chose to surrender his immortality in exchange for freeing Prometheus from his eagle, accepting death as the only cure for endless pain. He died for someone else.
facts.statement: Chiron was the one centaur who defied his species' reputation — wise, gentle, immortal — and died not in battle but from an accidental wound so he could free another's suffering.
facts.wowFactor: Chiron could not die from his wound but could not heal from it either — immortality as infinite suffering. He solved it by giving his immortality away to free Prometheus. The wisest being in Greek mythology ended his existence through voluntary self-sacrifice, choosing death over endless agony, and making someone else's liberation the price of his own.
facts.visualDescription: Old centaur seated on a hillside overlooking the Aegean at dusk, a young dark-haired boy beside him holding a small bow, medicinal herbs spread between them on a flat stone, scrolls and a lyre nearby — and on the centaur's left flank, just barely visible in the fading light, a wound that will not close

variants.question: The wise centaur Chiron tutored Achilles in warrior arts — what subject did he teach Jason?
How was Chiron mortally wounded despite being immortal?
variants.correctAnswer: A Hydra-poisoned arrow from Heracles grazed him by accident
distractors: He was struck by Zeus's lightning in the Titanomachy
He was bitten by the Nemean Lion
He fell fighting the Lapiths at the wedding battle
He was poisoned by Medea's herbs

variants.question: True or false: Chiron was a centaur known for wisdom who tutored heroes — unlike the savage behavior of other centaurs.
The wise centaur who tutored Achilles, Jason, and Heracles was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Trapped between a deadly whirlpool and a six-headed sea monster, Odysseus chose to pass which creature — losing six men but saving the ship?
facts.explanation: Circe briefed Odysseus on the strait's mechanics with brutal clarity: Charybdis swallowed the sea three times daily, and any ship caught in the vortex would lose everyone. Scylla had six heads and took one sailor per head per pass. The math was grim but simple. Odysseus chose Scylla and told his crew nothing — he had learned from the Sirens that foreknowledge of death causes panic that makes death more likely. He stood at the bow watching as each of Scylla's six heads took a man, the men crying his name as they were lifted screaming from the ship. He called it the most pitiable thing he saw in all his wandering. The strait appears in The Odyssey sub-deck as the most concentrated expression of Odysseus's commander's burden: sacrificing the few to save the many, in full knowledge and full silence.
facts.statement: Odysseus chose to sail near Scylla rather than risk Charybdis — knowing he would lose six men, he kept that calculation from his crew.
facts.wowFactor: Odysseus watched six men die — men he knew by name, who had survived Troy and years at sea with him — and did not move. He described their faces and screams in the Odyssey in more detail than almost any other moment of violence in the poem. He stayed at the bow because running to help them would have panicked the oarsmen and driven the ship into Charybdis. Command sometimes means watching.
facts.visualDescription: Ship mid-passage through a narrow dark strait, six long pale necks descending from a sea cliff on the right, each one with a screaming sailor in its teeth, the ship's oarsmen pulling desperately with eyes forward, and across the water on the left a massive circular black whirlpool slowly opening its drain

variants.question: Odysseus passed Scylla rather than Charybdis — what made Charybdis so deadly?
variants.correctAnswer: It was a whirlpool that could swallow the entire ship
distractors: It had more heads than Scylla
It breathed fire that could incinerate the sails
It was a serpent that coiled around ships and crushed them
It sang a song that made sailors leap overboard

variants.question: Who advised Odysseus to pass Scylla rather than Charybdis, accepting the loss of six men?
True or false: Odysseus chose to pass Scylla rather than Charybdis, losing six men but saving the ship.
The six-headed monster that Odysseus sailed past — sacrificing six men — was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which wind-spirit creatures tormented the blind prophet Phineus by stealing and fouling his food, and were chased away by the Argonauts?
facts.explanation: Phineus had been given prophecy by Zeus and misused it, revealing divine plans too precisely. His punishment was perpetual starvation: every time he sat to eat, the Harpies — wind spirits with women's faces and birds' bodies — descended to steal the food or foul it with their touch, leaving him too hungry to die and too tormented to live. Calais and Zetes, the winged Argonaut brothers born of the North Wind, chased the Harpies to the ends of the earth (stopping only when a divine messenger warned them not to kill the creatures). Phineus, free at last, repaid the Argonauts with the navigational intelligence that saved their entire quest — including how to pass the Symplegades, the Clashing Rocks. A freed old prophet's gratitude was worth more than any map.
facts.statement: The Harpies tormented blind prophet Phineus by stealing his every meal — until the Argonauts' winged sons of the North Wind chased them to the ends of the earth.
facts.wowFactor: The Argonauts freed Phineus as an act of compassion — they had no strategic reason to. In return, his sailing advice navigated them through obstacles that would otherwise have destroyed the expedition. The entire Golden Fleece quest succeeds in part because some heroes stopped to help a starving old man that everyone else had walked past.
facts.visualDescription: Open terrace above a grey sea, a frail blind old man raising both arms toward a sky full of screaming bird-women, food and pottery scattered across the floor, two armored figures with wings erupting from their backs launching upward in pursuit, the Harpies already shrinking to specks at the horizon

variants.question: The Harpies tormented Phineus by stealing his food — which two Argonauts chased them away?
variants.correctAnswer: Calais and Zetes, sons of the North Wind

variants.question: Why was the prophet Phineus being punished by Zeus with the Harpies?
variants.correctAnswer: He revealed too much of the gods' plans with his gift of prophecy
distractors: He refused to sacrifice to Zeus
He insulted Poseidon during a sea voyage
He blinded himself to see the future more clearly
He helped Medea escape from Colchis

variants.question: True or false: The Harpies tormented the blind prophet Phineus and were driven away by the Argonauts.
The winged spirits that stole the blind prophet Phineus's food were the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: According to myth, the Phoenix lived for how many years before bursting into flame and being reborn from its own ashes?
facts.explanation: The Phoenix was described as eagle-sized with plumage of burning red and gold, associated with Heliopolis, the Egyptian city of the sun. At the end of its 500-year cycle it gathered aromatic wood — cinnamon, nard, myrrh — built a nest, exposed it to the midday sun, and burned entirely. From the ashes a new Phoenix emerged, identical and young. It then carried the remains of its predecessor to the Sun Temple at Heliopolis. Uniquely among mythological creatures, the Phoenix was neither monster nor divine servant but an autonomous embodiment of cyclical time — and one of the few creatures whose story traveled intact across Greek, Egyptian, Persian, Chinese, and Arabian mythologies, each culture finding the same image essential.
facts.statement: The Phoenix lived exactly 500 years, then built its own funeral pyre and was reborn from the ashes — the only creature that chose the moment of its own death.
facts.wowFactor: The Phoenix myth appeared independently in ancient Egypt, Greece, Persia, Arabia, and China — cultures with limited contact producing the same bird, the same fire, the same resurrection. It is one of the most globally distributed single images in human mythology. The concept of death-as-renewal through fire may be too fundamental to human experience to need transmission.
facts.visualDescription: A vast bird made entirely of living flame — red at the wingtips bleeding into gold at the breast — nested in a pyramid of smoking cinnamon and myrrh sticks, the fire not burning the bird but being the bird, and below the nest's edge in the ash a single small feather that is already warm and beginning to curl upward

variants.question: The Phoenix lived 500 years and was reborn from what?
distractors: The tears of the sun god

variants.question: Where was the legendary home of the Phoenix associated with in ancient sources?
distractors: The Island of the Sun in the far east

variants.question: True or false: The Phoenix was reborn from its own ashes every 500 years.
The Phoenix was reborn from its own ashes every ___ years.

facts.quizQuestion: The Nemean Lion had skin impenetrable to all weapons — how did Heracles kill it for his first labor?
facts.explanation: Heracles's first labor established the template for everything that followed: impossible task, divine solution, transformation of the victory into a permanent tool. Arrows bounced off the golden pelt, spear shafts shattered, swords bent. He drove the lion into its cave and strangled it, then discovered nothing he owned was sharp enough to cut its skin. Only the lion's own bronze-hard claws could penetrate its hide. He wore the pelt as his defining symbol — it was already appearing on Heracles vase paintings before the canonical twelve labors were even fully established. The lion itself was another Echidna-Typhon offspring (see myth_typhon_father), meaning the first thing Heracles killed in his heroic career was his enemy's oldest child.
facts.statement: The Nemean Lion's hide turned every weapon harmlessly aside — so Heracles killed it with his hands, then used its own claws to skin it, and wore it as indestructible armor for every labor after.
facts.wowFactor: Heracles tried arrows first. They bounced. He tried a sword. It bent. He eventually walked into the cave after the lion and strangled it with his arms. Then he spent some time figuring out how to skin something nothing could cut, until he noticed the claws. The greatest hero in mythology's first major triumph involved a significant amount of improvisation.
facts.visualDescription: Hero and lion locked together in the mouth of a hillside cave, the hero's forearms wrapped around the lion's neck from behind, the lion's massive golden paws scrabbling at the stone floor, both figures straining in complete silence — no weapons anywhere in the frame

variants.question: Heracles strangled the Nemean Lion — what made its skin impossible to pierce with normal weapons?
variants.correctAnswer: Its golden fur was impenetrable to all weapons
distractors: It was protected by Hera's divine blessing
It had swallowed a magical stone
Its skin had been hardened by Poseidon's sea water
It had rolled in the ashes of the Underworld

variants.question: What did Heracles do with the Nemean Lion's skin after killing it?
variants.correctAnswer: Wore it as indestructible armor for his remaining labors
distractors: Presented it to King Eurystheus as proof
Burned it in honor of Zeus
Gave it to Athena for her shield
Buried it at the lion's den

variants.question: True or false: Heracles killed the Nemean Lion by strangling it since its skin repelled all weapons.
Unable to pierce the Nemean Lion's skin, Heracles killed it by ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Heracles scared the man-eating Stymphalian Birds into the sky using what noisemaker given by Athena?
facts.explanation: The Stymphalian Birds had bronze beaks, claws, and feathers they could launch like arrows. They had colonized the marshes of Lake Stymphalia so thoroughly that Heracles could not wade in to engage them. Athena provided a krotala — a bronze rattle or castanet — whose specific frequency startled them into flight, allowing Heracles to switch to his bow. The solution is notable: not brute force, not divine sanction, not intelligence, but the right tool for the right medium. The birds who survived the arrows were said to have fled to the Black Sea island of Aretias, where the Argonauts later encountered them — a cross-story connection threading through the Heroes and Monsters sub-decks.
facts.statement: Heracles drove the man-eating metal birds of Stymphalia into the air with divine bronze castanets — then shot them as they fled, completing his sixth labor.
facts.wowFactor: The greatest hero of the ancient world's sixth labor was won with a noisemaker. Athena, goddess of wisdom and strategy, correctly identified that the problem was not the birds' strength but their terrain — and the solution was not combat but acoustics. Sometimes the right answer is to make a very loud noise and wait.
facts.visualDescription: Hero at the marsh edge at dawn, bronze rattle raised high in one fist rattling furiously, the entire surface of the lake erupting upward in a storm of metallic wings catching the early light, bronze feathers falling like blades around him, his bow already in his other hand coming up

variants.question: The Stymphalian Birds had bronze beaks and feathers — what made them especially dangerous beyond their claws?
variants.correctAnswer: They could shoot their bronze feathers as projectiles
distractors: They could freeze prey with cold breath
Their gaze caused paralysis like Medusa's

variants.question: Slaying the Stymphalian Birds was which of Heracles's Twelve Labors?
True or false: Heracles used bronze castanets from Athena to scare the Stymphalian Birds into the air so he could shoot them.
Athena gave Heracles ___ to frighten the Stymphalian Birds into flight.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mythological creature with an eagle's head and a lion's body was said to guard gold treasure in remote mountains?
facts.explanation: The Griffin sat at the intersection of two ancient Greek symbols of supreme power: the eagle (Zeus's bird, king of the sky) and the lion (king of the earth). Ancient accounts placed them in the far Scythian north, where they hoarded gold and fought constant wars against the one-eyed Arimaspi people who coveted it. The image entered Persian art, spread to India, and became one of the most enduring creatures in European heraldry — appearing on royal coats of arms, church carvings, and university emblems for two millennia after the Greeks who invented it. It is one of the few mythological creatures with a plausible physical origin: some paleontologists suggest the beaked skull of a Protoceratops dinosaur, exposed by erosion in Scythian gold-mining country, may have been the visual source.
facts.statement: The Griffin — eagle's head and wings on a lion's body — guarded mountains of gold in the far north, a symbol so compelling it spread from Greece to Egypt, Persia, and medieval Europe.
facts.wowFactor: A Scythian gold-mining tradition met a dinosaur skull exposed by erosion, and the Greeks — hearing stories from traders — assembled them into the Griffin. It became the most widely reproduced heraldic creature in Western history. A fossil and a rumor built an immortal symbol.
facts.visualDescription: Enormous creature on a mountain peak, eagle's golden head angled downward toward a man-sized nugget of raw gold, lion's haunches braced against the wind, wings spread for balance, the flat steppe stretching to the horizon far below in every direction, another Griffin visible soaring in the distance

variants.question: The Griffin guarded gold from thieves — which one-eyed people were said to constantly battle Griffins for their gold?
What two animals made up the Griffin's body — symbolizing the kings of sky and earth?
True or false: The Griffin had an eagle's head and wings combined with a lion's body.
The creature with an eagle's head and lion's body that guarded gold was the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hundred-headed dragon guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides, killed or bypassed by Heracles for his eleventh labor?
facts.explanation: The golden apples were a wedding gift from Gaia to Hera — tended by the Hesperides nymphs and guarded by Ladon, who never slept. Heracles's eleventh labor required him to fetch them. The more famous version involves no combat at all: finding Atlas nearby, still holding the sky as his eternal punishment, Heracles offered to take the weight while Atlas retrieved the apples. Atlas returned with the apples and attempted to walk away, leaving Heracles stuck. Heracles asked him to hold it 'just for a moment' while he adjusted a shoulder pad — then walked away with the apples. Ladon was later placed by Zeus among the stars as the constellation Draco, one of the largest constellations visible in the northern sky.
facts.statement: Ladon was the hundred-headed dragon guarding Hera's golden apple tree — and Heracles either killed him or tricked Atlas into harvesting the apples while Heracles briefly held up the sky.
facts.wowFactor: Heracles outsmarted the literal holder of the heavens through a fake shoulder-pad adjustment. Atlas had endured the sky's weight for eternity, was offered a brief reprieve, and was immediately tricked by a hero pretending to be uncomfortable. The eleventh labor was won by the most implausible con in mythology.
facts.visualDescription: Garden at the edge of the world, evening light that seems older than ordinary sunset: a massive serpentine dragon coiled through the branches of a silver tree hung with glowing golden spheres, each of a hundred heads angled outward in a different direction, watching every approach simultaneously, the Hesperides visible as faint luminous shapes between the trees

variants.question: Ladon guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides — fetching them was which of Heracles's Twelve Labors?
After being killed or bypassed by Heracles, Ladon was said to have been transformed into what?
True or false: Ladon was a many-headed dragon who guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides.
The dragon guarding the golden apples of the Hesperides was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Orpheus descended into the Underworld to rescue his wife, who had died from a snakebite on their wedding day. Who was she?
facts.explanation: Eurydice was a dryad or mountain nymph. The shepherd Aristaeus — a minor god of beekeeping and cheese-making — pursued her on the day of her marriage to Orpheus. She fled, stepped on a serpent hiding in the grass, and died from its venom before her wedding night ended. Aristaeus was later punished for his part in her death: his bees all died and he had to perform ritual sacrifices to appease the gods and restore his swarms. Eurydice herself had no agency in her death or her near-resurrection — she was the object of others' desires and others' quests. Greek mythology rarely granted women the heroic narrative; Psyche's story in this sub-deck is the notable exception.
facts.statement: Eurydice died on her own wedding day, from a snakebite while fleeing a man who wanted her — and this is what sent Orpheus into the Underworld.
facts.wowFactor: The entire Orpheus myth — his descent, the silencing of Tartarus, Hades's tears, the failed condition — happened because a man who made bees and cheese couldn't take no for an answer on a wedding day. The greatest love story in Greek mythology began as the consequence of a minor god's harassment.
facts.visualDescription: A meadow in late afternoon, white flowers, scattered petals from a bridal wreath, a woman collapsed with one arm still reaching forward toward a path now empty, the small mark on her ankle barely visible, a man's retreating footprints in the soft earth behind her but no Aristaeus visible — he was already gone

variants.question: What caused the death of Eurydice, wife of Orpheus?
variants.correctAnswer: A snakebite on her wedding day

variants.question: Who was Eurydice fleeing when she was fatally bitten by a serpent?
True or false: Eurydice died from a snakebite on her wedding day to Orpheus.
The wife of Orpheus who died on their wedding day was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Orpheus's lyre music was so beautiful in the Underworld that which god wept iron tears and granted his request?
facts.explanation: The Erinyes (Furies) were the most pitiless beings in the cosmos — older than the Olympians, born from the blood of Uranus, designed specifically to pursue and torment those who violated blood bonds without rest or compassion. They had witnessed every atrocity since the beginning of the world and felt nothing. When Orpheus played, they wept. Hades — described across ancient sources as unyielding, implacable, never once bending his law — shed tears of iron. The entire punitive infrastructure of the Underworld paused: Sisyphus sat on his boulder, Ixion's wheel stopped, Tantalus let the water rise. One musician's grief temporarily suspended the entire moral economy of death. It was the most powerful thing Orpheus ever did, and it almost worked.
facts.statement: Orpheus's music made the Furies cry for the first time in their existence — and moved Hades, who had never shown mercy once in all of time, to grant it.
facts.wowFactor: The Furies had punished souls without pause since the beginning of time and had never once cried. Sisyphus had been rolling his boulder for eternity without a rest. These were not metaphors — these were the fixed, eternal mechanics of the Greek cosmos. One musician stopped all of it. Temporarily.
facts.visualDescription: Underworld great hall: stone columns hung with black chains, the whole space in a hush that has never existed before, three winged women with snake-hair in postures of open weeping, hands raised to their faces — and in the center of the room a single young man with a lyre that is still vibrating, kneeling

variants.question: What condition did Hades give Orpheus when allowing Eurydice to return?
variants.correctAnswer: He must not look back until they reach the surface
distractors: He must sing for one year in the Underworld
He must leave his lyre behind

variants.question: Which goddess, co-ruler of the Underworld, was also moved by Orpheus's music?
True or false: Hades agreed to release Eurydice after being moved by Orpheus's music.
The ruler of the Underworld who wept iron tears at Orpheus's music was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero lost his wife forever because he turned to look back at her on the final step out of the Underworld?
facts.explanation: The path out of the Underworld in Greek geography was a long, upward passage through dark tunnels — no landmarks, no light, no sound but his own footsteps and, he hoped, hers behind him. He could not verify she was following without breaking the condition. Some ancient commentators read his backward glance as a failure of faith; others read it as a rational response to uncertainty (how could he know she was truly there?); others read it as love overwhelming discipline — the need to see her face stronger than the need to bring her home. All three readings are simultaneously supported by the text. The ambiguity is the point: he failed, and the reason does not make it hurt less.
facts.statement: Orpheus led Eurydice out of death itself — and lost her again because doubt crept in on the last step before sunlight.
facts.wowFactor: He could hear nothing, feel nothing, know nothing — just walk forward in darkness trusting that the word of a god who had never kept a mercy before was keeping one now. That is a different kind of courage than fighting monsters. He almost had it.
facts.visualDescription: Underground tunnel, low and close, just beginning to lighten ahead with the ghost of day — a man's silhouette walking toward the light, one hand rising toward his shoulder in the beginning of a turn, and behind him a space that is already emptier than it was, the air already settling back into its natural state of nothing

variants.question: Why did Orpheus fail to bring Eurydice back from the dead?
variants.correctAnswer: He looked back at her before reaching the surface
distractors: He forgot the payment for Charon
He tried to fight the Furies
He broke his promise to Hades

variants.question: At what moment did Orpheus look back at Eurydice, dooming her?
variants.correctAnswer: The last step before reaching the surface
distractors: At the gates of the Underworld

variants.question: True or false: Orpheus lost Eurydice forever because he looked back on their way out of the Underworld.
Orpheus lost Eurydice by looking ___ at the last moment leaving Hades.

facts.quizQuestion: After losing Eurydice a second time, Orpheus rejected women and was eventually torn apart by which frenzied female followers of Dionysus?
facts.explanation: Orpheus turned from women after his second loss of Eurydice and sang only to young men — an affront the Maenads, female devotees of Dionysus, took personally. They tore him apart in a ritual frenzy, scattering his limbs. But his severed head, still singing, floated down the river Hebrus and across the sea to the island of Lesbos, where it was enshrined. Lesbos subsequently became the center of lyric poetry in the ancient world — producing Sappho — and some ancient authors saw a direct connection: Orpheus's continued music after death consecrated the island to song. His lyre was placed among the stars as the constellation Lyra.
facts.statement: After losing Eurydice twice, Orpheus mourned alone — and was torn apart by the Maenads, whose rage he survived as a singing head that floated all the way to Lesbos.
facts.wowFactor: Orpheus's severed head kept singing after the body was gone. It floated down a river and across the sea, still making music, and washed up on an island that became the birthplace of lyric poetry. He was more powerful as a disembodied head than most poets are alive.
facts.visualDescription: Wide flat river at night, moonlit, moving slowly: on the dark water a severed head with closed eyes, expression not of horror but of grief and resignation, and from its open mouth a faint visible shimmer of sound moving outward across the water like rings, the riverbank behind it showing broken things in the grass

variants.question: What happened to Orpheus's severed head after the Maenads killed him?
variants.correctAnswer: It floated down a river still singing
distractors: Zeus turned it into a constellation

variants.question: The Maenads who killed Orpheus were followers of which god?
True or false: Orpheus was torn apart by the Maenads after he rejected all women following Eurydice's second death.
Orpheus was killed by the ___, frenzied female worshippers of Dionysus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess became jealous of the mortal Psyche, whose beauty was so great that people were neglecting the goddess's own temples?
facts.explanation: Psyche's beauty was not merely admired — it was creating a religious crisis. Worshippers were abandoning Aphrodite's temples, leaving offerings and prayers at Psyche's feet instead. This was not flattery to a god but theft from one. Aphrodite sent Eros to make Psyche fall obsessively in love with the most wretched creature alive — a punishment through desire rather than pain. But Eros, approaching the sleeping girl to loose his golden arrow, accidentally grazed himself with it. He fell in love with Psyche and arranged for her to be taken to his hidden palace instead. The plan to use love as a weapon against a mortal backfired through the very mechanism of that weapon. Aphrodite's jealousy threads directly through myth_psyche_lamp_wax, myth_psyche_tasks, and myth_psyche_goddess in this sub-deck.
facts.statement: Aphrodite was so enraged by a mortal whose beauty was causing her temples to be abandoned that she sent her own son to destroy her — and he fell in love instead.
facts.wowFactor: Aphrodite's plan to weaponize desire against Psyche was undone by desire itself. The god of love, sent to make someone love the wrong thing, made himself love the right person. The goddess of love was defeated by love. Her own son's nature was the counterargument to her jealousy.
facts.visualDescription: Stone roadside shrine with wilting flowers and fresh offerings, the old temple of Aphrodite visible behind it cracked and overgrown, its doors hanging open, and in the shrine's center a simple stone carving of a woman's face — beautiful in the way that makes people forget where they were going

variants.question: Why did Aphrodite send Eros to curse the mortal Psyche?
variants.correctAnswer: Psyche's beauty caused people to neglect Aphrodite's temples
distractors: Psyche was a priestess of Hera
Psyche had stolen Aphrodite's golden apple
Zeus had praised Psyche over Aphrodite

variants.question: What task did Aphrodite give Eros concerning Psyche?
variants.correctAnswer: Make her fall in love with something hideous

variants.question: True or false: Aphrodite became jealous of Psyche because mortals worshipped Psyche's beauty instead of the goddess.
The goddess who was jealous of Psyche's beauty and plotted against her was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mortal woman accidentally dripped hot lamp oil on her sleeping divine husband, causing him to flee, because she couldn't resist seeing his face?
facts.explanation: Eros had kept his identity from Psyche for good reason: his mother Aphrodite would have been apoplectic. He visited only in darkness and forbade her from looking at his face, telling her she was married to a divine power who would leave if she broke that trust. Her sisters, jealous, suggested her husband was a horrible monster hiding in the dark, poisoning her mind with the one thing more potent than desire: doubt. She lit the lamp. What she saw was not a monster but a god of devastating beauty — golden-winged, young, peaceful in sleep. Her hand shook; the oil dropped. The burn woke him. He fled not from anger but from the knowledge that what was between them was now broken by exactly the kind of doubt he had asked her not to have.
facts.statement: Psyche held a lamp over her sleeping husband expecting a monster — and found a god so beautiful she trembled, and a single drop of oil woke him.
facts.wowFactor: Psyche's sisters invented the monster out of pure spite — and the invented monster almost worked better than a real one. Psyche survived gods, impossible tasks, and the Underworld. She nearly destroyed everything over a lie her sisters told her in an afternoon. The threat that almost ended the myth was human jealousy, not divine wrath.
facts.visualDescription: A white bedroom at deepest night, a golden-winged figure asleep on the bed, and a woman leaning over him with a small oil lamp, her face caught between wonder and terror, the oil already leaving the lamp's lip in a single gleaming drop that catches the light as it falls — and the sleeper's eyes beginning to open

variants.question: What caused Eros to flee from Psyche during their secret marriage?
variants.correctAnswer: She dripped lamp oil on him while secretly looking at his face
distractors: She told her family his true identity
She prayed to Aphrodite against him

variants.question: Who convinced Psyche to look at her sleeping husband, setting the tragedy in motion?
True or false: Psyche lit a lamp to see Eros's face while he slept, accidentally burning him with hot oil.
Psyche drove Eros away by dripping hot ___ on him while he slept.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess gave Psyche a series of impossible tasks — including sorting a mountain of seeds and fetching beauty from the Underworld — to win back Eros?
facts.explanation: The four tasks were designed to kill or break Psyche: sort an insurmountable mountain of mixed seeds by dawn (an army of ants sorted them), gather golden wool from the violent rams of the sun (a reed advised her to collect it from the bushes where fleece had caught), fill a crystal vial from the river Styx's source (an eagle of Zeus carried the vial to the source and back), and descend to the Underworld to retrieve beauty from Persephone (she used the cheat code described in myth_cerberus_psyche in the Monsters sub-deck). Each task was impossible for a mortal alone. Each time, part of the natural world — animal, plant, bird, river — intervened to help her. Ancient interpreters read this as the universe's endorsement: Psyche's love was genuine and the cosmos acknowledged it.
facts.statement: Aphrodite gave Psyche four impossible tasks to win Eros back — and the universe kept helping her, as if love itself was bending the world in her favor.
facts.wowFactor: Aphrodite sent Psyche into the Underworld expecting her to die. Psyche came back. She then opened the box of beauty on the return trip out of exhausted vanity — wanting to look good for Eros — and collapsed. The Underworld was the task she survived; her own impulse was the trap she fell into. Eros retrieved her.
facts.visualDescription: A woman standing knee-deep in a stream at the base of an impossible cliff where dark water pours from a crack in the rock, a massive eagle descending toward her with a crystal vial in its talons, her face upturned in gratitude and exhaustion, the task almost done, one more impossible thing almost accomplished

variants.question: One of Aphrodite's tasks for Psyche involved retrieving beauty from the Underworld — from which goddess?
Who helped Psyche sort the mountain of mixed seeds in her first impossible task?
True or false: Aphrodite gave Psyche impossible tasks including fetching beauty from the Underworld.
After losing Eros, Psyche completed impossible tasks set by ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mortal woman completed Aphrodite's impossible tasks, was reunited with Eros, and was made an immortal goddess by Zeus?
facts.explanation: After Eros retrieved the collapsed Psyche and presented his case to Zeus, Zeus granted her immortality — she drank ambrosia and was transformed from mortal to divine. She and Eros married formally and had a daughter, Hedone (Pleasure). The word psyche means soul in Greek, and the Psyche myth is one of the oldest surviving allegories in Western literature: the soul (Psyche), separated from love (Eros) by doubt and divine interference, undergoes trials that make it worthy of reunion with love, and is elevated to the divine in the process. The myth parallels in structure the Norse Valkyrie who chooses a mortal hero and defies Odin — both stories about a divine being who risks everything to love across the boundary that separates god from mortal.
facts.statement: Psyche is the only mortal in Greek mythology to become a goddess through love and perseverance alone — her name means 'soul,' and the myth is the soul's journey to divine love.
facts.wowFactor: Psyche earned divinity. Not by being born to a god, not by performing labors assigned by a king, not by winning a war — by choosing to love someone dangerous, surviving every punishment jealousy could devise, and coming back from death with a box in her hands. Her name means soul. She became a goddess by having one.
facts.visualDescription: Open sky at the edge of Olympus — not the mountain, the actual heaven — a young woman with butterfly wings still wet from their first unfurling, reaching upward toward a golden-winged god who reaches down, both of them in the moment before their hands meet, cloud and starlight and vast distance between them and below them the whole world

variants.question: What does the Greek word 'psyche' (Psyche's name) mean?
What was the name of Psyche and Eros's daughter?
True or false: Psyche became a goddess after completing Aphrodite's tasks and was given ambrosia by Zeus.
The mortal woman elevated to goddess by Zeus after her trials was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hero was told by the Oracle at Delphi that he would kill his father and marry his mother, leading to his parents abandoning him as an infant?
facts.explanation: The Oracle at Delphi told Laius and Jocasta that their son would kill his father and marry his mother. Their solution — infanticide — was reasonable by the logic of the time. They pierced the baby's ankles to prevent his ghost from walking (giving him the name Oedipus, 'swollen foot'), gave him to a shepherd to expose on Mount Cithaeron, and considered the threat eliminated. The shepherd instead gave the baby to a Corinthian herdsman, who brought him to the childless king and queen of Corinth, who raised him as their own. When Oedipus grew up and heard a rumor he might be adopted, he consulted the Oracle himself — who told him only the prophecy about killing his father and marrying his mother. He fled Corinth to avoid this, heading straight toward Thebes. The name he carried — his injury — was the mark of the fate his parents had tried to cancel.
facts.statement: Oedipus's parents pierced his ankles and left him to die on a mountain to prevent the Oracle's prophecy — and every step they took to escape it moved it closer.
facts.wowFactor: Every act of prevention was a step toward fulfillment. The shepherd's mercy delivered the baby to Corinth. Oedipus's flight from Corinth aimed him at Thebes. His search for truth at the Oracle guaranteed he'd hear exactly what sent him on the road where his father was traveling. The prophecy didn't overcome their precautions — it used them.
facts.visualDescription: Rocky hillside at dawn, a shepherd standing over a swaddled infant whose ankles are tied with a cord of reed, staring down at the child with an expression that is deciding something, the distant city of Thebes a pale smear on the plain below, the shepherd's hand already moving toward the cord

variants.question: What does the name 'Oedipus' mean, and why was he given it?
variants.correctAnswer: Swollen foot — his ankles were pierced and bound as an infant
distractors: Cursed child — for the prophecy he carried
Wandering king — for his long exile
Blind one — for gouging out his eyes
Riddle solver — for defeating the Sphinx

variants.question: In which city was Oedipus raised after being rescued, believing his adoptive parents were his real parents?
True or false: Oedipus's parents tried to kill him as an infant to prevent the oracle's prophecy from coming true.
The oracle prophesied that Oedipus would kill his ___ and marry his mother.

facts.quizQuestion: What creature blocked the road to Thebes and devoured travelers who could not answer its riddle, until Oedipus solved it?
facts.explanation: The Sphinx had terrorized Thebes for years, killing every traveler who failed her riddle. Oedipus arrived at the road to Thebes having just fled Corinth to avoid his prophecy, having just unknowingly killed a man at a crossroads (his actual father Laius), and traveling alone. He faced the Sphinx without help, without divine armor, with only intelligence. His answer — man — is a meditation on human time: the same creature on all fours, then two feet, then three. Thebes rewarded him with the throne vacated by the recently-murdered Laius and the hand of the widowed queen Jocasta. Oedipus, who understood human life's arc from crawling to the grave, walked directly into the worst horror of that life without understanding his own story at all. The Sphinx is inseparable from the myth_sphinx_riddle entry in the Monsters sub-deck.
facts.statement: Oedipus answered the Sphinx's riddle with 'man' — ending the monster's reign — and Thebes rewarded him with its throne and its widowed queen: his mother.
facts.wowFactor: He solved the riddle about human life while in the middle of a human life that would end in the most extreme violation of human bonds. The answer was 'man' and he was the man. The irony is not tragic in the Greek sense — it is the structure of the entire myth.
facts.visualDescription: Mountain road at midday, the Sphinx on her cliff looking down at a solitary traveler who has stopped and tilted his head to think, the sun harsh and absolute, the bones of previous travelers pale on the slope below, the Sphinx's expression for the first time uncertain as the traveler's mouth opens with the answer

variants.question: What was the Sphinx's famous riddle that Oedipus solved?
variants.correctAnswer: What walks on four legs at dawn, two at noon, three at dusk?
distractors: What has roots as nobody sees, but taller than trees?
What has a heart that doesn't beat?
What is always before you but cannot be seen?
What speaks without a mouth and hears without ears?

variants.question: What happened to the Sphinx after Oedipus correctly answered her riddle?
variants.correctAnswer: She threw herself off a cliff and died
distractors: She shrank to a harmless creature

variants.question: True or false: Solving the Sphinx's riddle earned Oedipus the kingship of Thebes.
The creature whose riddle Oedipus solved to save Thebes was the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which king of Thebes gouged out his own eyes upon discovering he had unknowingly killed his father and married his own mother?
facts.explanation: The plague that struck Thebes was sent because a murderer walked free among the living. Oedipus, the king and detective, interrogated every witness, pressed every seer (Tiresias warned him to stop; he pressed on), followed every thread. Each piece of evidence was a step toward his own guilt. His wife Jocasta began to understand before he did and begged him to stop asking. He accused her of fearing he was low-born. She hanged herself in the palace while he was still questioning the shepherd who had been ordered to expose him as an infant. He found that shepherd, asked the final question, and received the final answer in the same moment. Jocasta was dead. He took her golden brooches and blinded himself. Then he went into exile, guided by his daughter Antigone (see myth_oedipus_antigone_exile in this sub-deck).
facts.statement: Oedipus investigating his own city's plague realized he was the murderer he was searching for — and kept digging until every terrible truth was confirmed.
facts.wowFactor: The investigation was perfect. He found every witness, refused every suggestion to stop, cross-referenced every account, and arrived at a complete, accurate picture of the truth. Then he blinded himself for having seen it. He was the best detective in Thebes and the worst possible person for the case.
facts.visualDescription: Palace central court in midday heat, a king in gold-hemmed robes on his knees in the dust, both hands pressed to his face and blood running between his fingers, servants frozen in the doorways, a woman's crown visible on the marble floor where it was dropped, the court's central altar still smoking from a now-irrelevant sacrifice

variants.question: What did Jocasta do when she realized the truth about her relationship with Oedipus?
distractors: She threw herself from the palace walls
She called on Hera for mercy

distractors: In a forest on Mount Cithaeron

variants.question: True or false: Oedipus gouged out his own eyes when he discovered he had fulfilled the terrible oracle.
