facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess cursed the nymph Echo to only be able to repeat the last words spoken to her, as punishment for distracting her from Zeus's affairs?
facts.explanation: Echo was a mountain nymph with an extraordinary gift for conversation who used it at Zeus's direction: she kept Hera engaged in long, absorbing talks while Zeus's divine affairs ended and evidence was hidden. When Hera understood what had happened, she could not punish Zeus (she never could, not really) and instead destroyed what had been used against her: Echo's voice. The curse was surgical — Echo could not initiate speech, could not say her own words, could only repeat the final syllables others spoke. She then met Narcissus, fell in love, could not tell him so, could only echo his words back to him — which to him sounded like mockery. She faded until only her voice remained, a phenomenon that still carries her name in physics, architecture, and radar.
facts.statement: Hera cursed Echo not for her own sin but for being Zeus's cover story — stripping her of speech except the last words others said.
facts.wowFactor: Echo was punished for being useful to Zeus. She lost her voice — her only distinct characteristic, the thing that made her herself — for serving as cover for someone else's sin. Her punishment then interacted with Narcissus's punishment to produce a double tragedy: two people in the same woods, neither able to reach the other, her by curse, him by nature.
facts.visualDescription: Mountain slope in dusk, the nymph reduced to translucency against the stone cliff face, barely a silhouette, only her mouth still clearly defined, open and forming words that are someone else's words, her hands pressed flat against the cliff as if leaning into it is the last physical thing she can do

variants.question: Why did Hera curse Echo with the inability to speak original words?
variants.correctAnswer: Echo distracted Hera with conversation while Zeus's nymph companions escaped
distractors: Echo had fallen in love with Zeus
Echo had stolen Hera's golden crown
Echo had spread gossip about Hera on Olympus

variants.question: What type of nymph was Echo — associated with which natural feature?
True or false: Hera cursed Echo so she could only repeat the last words spoken to her.
The nymph Echo was cursed by ___ to only repeat others' words.

facts.quizQuestion: Which beautiful Greek youth fell in love with his own reflection in a pool and wasted away gazing at it, giving us a word still used today?
facts.explanation: Narcissus was a hunter from Thespiae of supernatural beauty. He rejected everyone who loved him, male and female alike, and their prayers for justice reached Nemesis — goddess of retribution against excessive pride. She led him to a pool of perfectly still water where he saw his own reflection for the first time and fell immediately, overwhelmingly in love. He could not leave. He could not embrace the face. He recognized eventually that it was his own reflection and still could not stop staring. He wasted away beside the pool and died, becoming the narcissus flower. The word narcissism — a clinical diagnosis describing pathological self-focus at others' expense — comes from his name, as does the word 'narcotic' (though through a different Greek root).
facts.statement: Narcissus rejected every lover — including the voice-cursed Echo — until the gods punished him by making him fall in love with the one face he could never reach: his own.
facts.wowFactor: Narcissus and Echo occupy the same story: she could only repeat his words, he could only love his own face. Two people in the same forest, both trapped in self-referential loops by different curses. Modern psychology named a personality disorder after him. Ancient mythology had already described it with more compassion than the diagnostic criteria.
facts.visualDescription: Forest pool in deep morning shadow, the water surface mirror-perfect, a beautiful young man lying full-length along the bank with one arm trailing in the water toward the reflection of his own arm reaching back, white narcissus flowers already beginning to bloom where his other hand presses the earth

variants.question: What modern psychological concept takes its name from the myth of Narcissus?
What flower did Narcissus become after wasting away at his reflection?
True or false: Narcissus rejected Echo's love and was punished by falling in love with his own reflection.
The word 'narcissism' comes from the myth of ___, who fell in love with his reflection.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Phrygian king wished that everything he touched would turn to gold — and then couldn't eat, drink, or embrace his daughter?
facts.explanation: Midas had been generous to the satyr Silenus, companion of Dionysus, who had gotten lost. Dionysus, grateful, offered any wish. Midas wished for the Midas touch: everything he touched would transform to gold. The wish was granted literally and completely. His bread hardened. His wine became liquid gold that burned his throat. His daughter, running to embrace him, became a golden statue in mid-step. He prayed to Dionysus to reverse the gift, and Dionysus instructed him to wash in the river Pactolus — which turned gold from his touch and became historically one of the richest sources of gold dust in the ancient world, explaining Lydia's famous wealth. Midas appears again in other myths with different follies; his golden touch story is the clearest ancient statement of the wish-gone-wrong pattern — a pattern also visible in Phaethon, Psyche, Pygmalion, and Pandora in this sub-deck.
facts.statement: Midas wished for the golden touch and got it — then watched his food turn to metal, his wine solidify, and his daughter, reaching up to him, become a statue.
facts.wowFactor: The myth is 3,000 years old and still the most efficient possible illustration of 'be careful what you wish for.' Every version, in every culture that adopted it, arrives at the daughter. That is not the most economically significant consequence or the most physically painful. It is the daughter, because she is the thing no amount of gold can replace.
facts.visualDescription: Royal garden, afternoon sun, a man in golden robes standing perfectly still before a golden statue of a girl — same height, same posture as a child running toward a parent — one of his hands hovering just above where her shoulder would be, not touching, never touching again, his face doing something that golden statues cannot do

variants.question: Which god granted King Midas the golden touch as a reward?
How did King Midas remove the curse of the golden touch?
variants.correctAnswer: He washed in the river Pactolus
distractors: He prayed at Apollo's temple for 10 days
He sacrificed his crown to Zeus
He gave away all his gold to the poor

variants.question: True or false: King Midas turned his daughter into gold and then begged Dionysus to reverse the golden touch.
King Midas's golden touch was granted by the god ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mortal weaver challenged Athena to a contest, produced a flawless tapestry showing the gods' misdeeds, and was turned into a spider?
facts.explanation: Arachne was a Lydian girl whose weaving was so extraordinary that people said she must have been taught by Athena herself. She denied it, boasted she was better than the goddess, and challenged her to a contest. Both wove; Athena depicted the gods' majesty and the punishment of mortal presumption. Arachne wove the gods' crimes — Zeus's rapes, Apollo's deceptions, Poseidon's violence — in images so perfectly executed that Athena could not fault the craft. She could only be enraged by the content and its quality combined. She tore the tapestry apart and struck Arachne. Arachne hanged herself from shame; Athena transformed her into a spider to weave forever, removing the shame of death but condemning her to eternal labor. Every spider on earth carries her name: arachnid.
facts.statement: Arachne's tapestry was technically perfect and politically devastating — every crime Zeus ever committed, woven flawlessly — and Athena tore it up and punished her for being right.
facts.wowFactor: Arachne was punished not for losing but for being undeniably right and technically perfect. Her tapestry was accurate — those things happened. Her craft was flawless — Athena could not find a flaw. The god of wisdom punished a mortal for telling the truth beautifully. Arachne got the last word: every web ever spun bears her name.
facts.visualDescription: A loom in a Lydian workshop, the tapestry still half-hanging from it showing a scene of unmistakable divine misconduct rendered in colors more vivid than real life — and beside it, a tall armored goddess mid-gesture of tearing, her expression not triumphant but something more complicated than that, the weaver already small in the doorframe behind her, walking toward a rope

variants.question: What is the word origin of 'arachnid' (the class containing spiders)?
variants.correctAnswer: Arachne, the mortal weaver turned into a spider by Athena
distractors: Araca, a Lydian word for silk

variants.question: What did Arachne's tapestry depict that so enraged Athena?
variants.correctAnswer: The gods' crimes, scandals, and deceptions
distractors: A scene of mortals superior to gods
Athena's defeat in the weaving contest

variants.question: True or false: Arachne was turned into a spider by Athena after a weaving contest, giving us the word 'arachnid'.
The mortal weaver challenged ___ to a contest and was transformed into a spider.

facts.quizQuestion: Which son of the sun god demanded to drive the solar chariot to prove his parentage, lost control, and had to be struck down by Zeus's thunderbolt?
facts.explanation: Phaethon's friends mocked his claim to divine parentage. He went to Helios, demanded proof, and asked to drive the solar chariot for one day. Helios had already sworn by the River Styx — an oath that even gods could not break — to grant whatever his son asked. He tried to talk Phaethon out of it, describing every danger of the route: the height, the horses' wildness, the constellations that would frighten them. Phaethon was unmoved. He took the reins, the horses sensed an unskilled hand and bolted, the chariot plunged low and scorched the earth (creating the Sahara in the myth), and Zeus struck him with a thunderbolt before the world could be destroyed. Phaethon fell burning into the river Eridanus; his sisters, mourning at the riverbank, were transformed into poplar trees that wept amber tears — the origin of amber in the myth.
facts.statement: Phaethon convinced his father Helios to let him drive the sun chariot for one day — and Helios, bound by an oath, watched his son burn to death unable to prevent it.
facts.wowFactor: Helios knew exactly what would happen. He told Phaethon precisely. He described the horses, the dangers, the route. He begged him to choose a different proof of parentage. He was bound by an oath and watched his son die anyway. The tragedy is not Phaethon's hubris — it's the father who saw it coming and was made to hand him the reins.
facts.visualDescription: A young man in a chariot built for a god, not a boy, the horses already running at angles no harness was made for, the earth impossibly close below and on fire in a widening trail, the sky behind him showing the proper path the sun should be taking, and in the near distance a stormcloud forming with something in it that is aimed directly at him

variants.question: Why did Zeus strike Phaethon with a thunderbolt?
variants.correctAnswer: Phaethon had lost control of the sun chariot and was scorching the earth
distractors: He had stolen the chariot without permission
He had challenged Zeus for control of Olympus
He had stolen fire from the gods like Prometheus
He had insulted Apollo at Delphi

variants.question: Why couldn't Helios refuse to let Phaethon drive the sun chariot?
variants.correctAnswer: He was bound by an oath he had made
distractors: Zeus had ordered him to grant the wish
The Fates had already written it
He believed Phaethon could handle it
Poseidon had bet it would happen

variants.question: True or false: Phaethon was struck down by Zeus's thunderbolt after losing control of the sun chariot and scorching the earth.
Phaethon lost control of the ___ chariot, his father's divine vehicle.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess answered Pygmalion's prayer and brought his ivory statue of the perfect woman to life?
facts.explanation: Pygmalion had rejected all real women and channeled his longing into ivory — carving a figure of impossible perfection, naming her Galatea, dressing her in jewels, giving her gifts as if she were alive. On Aphrodite's festival, surrounded by worshippers praying for what they desired, he prayed carefully: not for the statue to live, but for a wife like his ivory girl. Aphrodite heard the real prayer beneath the careful phrasing. When Pygmalion returned home and touched Galatea, the ivory was warm. It yielded under his thumb. She opened her eyes. The modern story of someone preferring an idealized version of a person to any real one — the Pygmalion Effect in psychology, Shaw's play, My Fair Lady — all descend from this myth. So does the engineering concept: the Pygmalion Effect describes how expectations, once formed, shape the reality they anticipate.
facts.statement: Pygmalion prayed on Aphrodite's festival day for a wife 'like his ivory girl' — and came home to find the statue warm.
facts.wowFactor: Pygmalion invented the person he could love because no real person fit what he needed. Aphrodite made the invention real. The myth is not a happy ending — it's a warning about what happens when an ideal becomes a person you have to live with. Shaw understood this. His Pygmalion ends with the sculptor losing control of his creation.
facts.visualDescription: Workshop at evening, a sculptor standing before a full-length ivory figure with one hand resting on its hand, looking down at the place where their skin meets with an expression of absolute stillness — not joy yet, but the intake of breath before joy, his fingers just beginning to register that the surface temperature is wrong in the right direction

variants.question: What was the name of the ivory statue that Aphrodite brought to life for Pygmalion?
On which divine festival did Pygmalion pray to Aphrodite for a wife like his statue?
True or false: Pygmalion fell in love with his own ivory sculpture, and Aphrodite granted his prayer to bring it to life.
Pygmalion's ivory statue was given life by the goddess ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god gave Cassandra the gift of true prophecy and then, when she rejected him, cursed her so no one would ever believe her?
facts.explanation: Cassandra was the Trojan princess who received genuine prophetic ability from Apollo in exchange for a promised favor. She took the gift and refused the favor. Apollo, unable to retract a divine gift, cursed its usefulness instead: every word she spoke would be true, and no one would believe any of it. She predicted the fall of Troy before Paris left for Sparta. She warned that the Trojan Horse was full of soldiers. She identified Agamemnon's murderer the day of his return. She was right about everything, always, and was dismissed as mad, attached to temple pillars in hysteria, dragged away in the moments her screaming was most accurate. She was killed immediately after Troy fell. The word 'Cassandra' entered English as a term for anyone who accurately predicts catastrophes no one heeds — a warning attached to the original warning.
facts.statement: Apollo gave Cassandra perfect prophecy, and when she refused him, added one condition: no one would ever believe her.
facts.wowFactor: Cassandra is mythology's most accurate prophet and its most ignored voice. She was right about everything — Troy fell exactly as she described — and died having been believed by no one in her entire life. Apollo's curse was not torture through pain but torture through clarity: she saw everything coming and could do nothing about any of it.
facts.visualDescription: Troy's walls at night, a woman with prophetic eyes wide and fixed on something the other figures cannot see, one arm pointing down at a wooden horse being trundled through the gate while around her people celebrate and turn from her, her mouth open in a warning that has the shape of a scream but the volume of someone no one is listening to

variants.question: Why did Apollo curse Cassandra so no one would believe her true prophecies?
variants.correctAnswer: She rejected his romantic advances after accepting his gift
distractors: She shared his prophecies publicly without permission
She worshipped Athena instead of Apollo
She told Trojans to surrender to the Greeks
She broke her oath as a priestess of Apollo

variants.question: Which specific warning did Cassandra give that was most famously ignored?
variants.correctAnswer: That the Trojan Horse contained Greek soldiers
distractors: That Paris would cause the Trojan War
That Achilles was invulnerable except his heel
That Agamemnon would be murdered at home
That the gods had abandoned Troy

variants.question: True or false: Cassandra's prophecies were always true, but Apollo cursed her so that no one would ever believe her.
Cassandra was cursed by ___ so that her true prophecies would never be believed.

facts.quizQuestion: Which powerful sorceress helped Jason get the Golden Fleece, fled Colchis with him, and later killed their own children to punish him for abandoning her?
facts.explanation: Medea had sacrificed everything for Jason: she had betrayed her father, killed her brother Absyrtus and scattered his body to delay pursuit, used her power to complete the impossible tasks in the Golden Fleece quest (myth_jason_medea_tasks, myth_medea_jason_fleece, both in this sub-deck), and made a new life in a country that was not hers. When Jason chose the Corinthian princess Glauce for political advancement, he offered Medea a reasonable arrangement by the standards of his time. She sent Glauce a wedding gift: a crown and robe anointed with poison. They burned through Glauce and her father King Creon when he tried to help her. Then Medea killed her sons by Jason — not in madness, not in grief, but in full knowledge that the deepest wound she could inflict was to leave him with no heirs, no legacy, no children to carry his name. She escaped on a dragon chariot to Athens. Jason died years later, alone, when the prow of his rotting ship fell on him.
facts.statement: Jason abandoned Medea for a political marriage after she had burned her bridges, killed her own brother, and given him everything — and she ensured he had no future left.
facts.wowFactor: Euripides's Medea (431 BCE) shocked Athens not because a woman had committed terrible acts — that existed in mythology — but because she did so with full agency and clear logic, no divine compulsion, no madness, no excuse. The audience was forced to follow her reasoning and understand it. She is the most morally complex character in Greek tragedy because her actions are understandable, not because they are justified.
facts.visualDescription: Palace corridor, a woman walking away from a room whose door she has just pulled shut, dragon-chariot visible through the colonnade window, and from under the door a light that is not lamp light — too steady, too wrong — and her face forward, not looking back, expression in its way more frightening than grief

variants.question: How did Medea kill Jason's new bride, the Corinthian princess?
variants.correctAnswer: A poisoned crown and robe that burned her alive
distractors: She sent poison wine to the wedding feast
She called down a curse from Hecate
She turned her to stone with dragon's teeth
She summoned a sea monster to destroy the palace

variants.question: How did Medea escape after killing her children and Jason's bride?
variants.correctAnswer: On a chariot pulled by dragons
distractors: She sailed to Athens on Jason's own ship
Hecate transported her to the Underworld
She transformed into a raven and flew away
She vanished into thin air using her sorcery

variants.question: True or false: Medea killed her own children and Jason's new bride after Jason abandoned her for a political marriage.
The sorceress who killed her children to punish Jason for his betrayal was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which daughter of Oedipus defied King Creon's order and buried her brother Polyneices, accepting death rather than leave him unburied?
facts.explanation: Antigone was Oedipus's daughter, born into a family already shattered by prophecy and truth. After her father's exile (myth_oedipus_antigone_exile in this sub-deck), she returned to Thebes where her brothers Eteocles and Polyneices killed each other in civil war. King Creon declared Eteocles a hero and Polyneices a traitor, ordering his body left to rot — an act that in Greek belief condemned his soul to wander the boundary of the Underworld forever, never reaching Elysium or the Asphodel Meadows (the Underworld sub-deck details this). Antigone performed burial rites. Creon condemned her to be sealed alive in a cave. She hanged herself inside it. Creon's son, her betrothed, killed himself at her body. His mother then killed herself. Creon survived to live with all of it. Sophocles wrote this in 441 BCE; it is still performed globally because its central question — what do you do when the law of the state contradicts what you know is right — has never been resolved.
facts.statement: Antigone chose to bury her brother knowing the law and knowing the consequence — and accepted being buried alive rather than leave him unburied in the dirt.
facts.wowFactor: Sophocles posed the Antigone question 2,500 years ago and every political generation since has believed it was written about them. It has been performed by resistance movements, by regimes, by every side of every major moral conflict in Western history. The question of whether divine law supersedes human law is still open.
facts.visualDescription: Dawn on a battlefield strewn with the aftermath of civil war, a young woman in plain dress kneeling beside a man's body, both hands cupped around a handful of earth which she pours slowly and deliberately over him, soldiers visible in the distance moving toward her, her eyes down and steady, not running

variants.question: What was Creon's punishment for Antigone defying his burial decree?
variants.correctAnswer: She was buried alive in a sealed tomb
distractors: She was exiled from Thebes forever
She was drowned in the river
She was stoned in the public square

variants.question: Antigone's story illustrates a conflict between which two types of law?
variants.correctAnswer: Divine moral law vs. human state law

variants.question: True or false: Antigone chose death over abandoning her religious duty to bury her brother.
Antigone was condemned for burying her brother ___, against King Creon's orders.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god of love had two types of arrows — gold-tipped to cause love and lead-tipped to cause hatred — and is called Cupid by the Romans?
facts.explanation: Eros (Cupid to the Romans) was more than a symbol of love — he was its unpredictable mechanism. The gold arrow struck whoever was in the target's sight next, causing immediate, overwhelming love. The lead arrow caused immediate, cold repulsion for whoever was next seen. He used both regularly at Aphrodite's direction but also according to his own whim. The Apollo-Daphne story — Apollo mocking Eros's archery, Eros shooting Apollo with gold and Daphne with lead to teach him humility — is the most famous example. Daphne prayed to her father Peneus to be transformed rather than caught; he changed her into a laurel tree, which became Apollo's sacred plant, its leaves used to crown victors at the Pythian Games. Love, in Greek mythology, is frequently an external force applied without consent — which is why Aphrodite and Eros together represent not romance but divine interference with mortal desire.
facts.statement: Eros carried two arrows — gold to ignite love, lead to cause aversion — and was not selective about where he aimed them.
facts.wowFactor: The Greeks did not experience love as something that arose naturally from within — they experienced it as something done to you from outside, by a god with arrows. The helplessness of falling in love, the irrationality of it, the way it overrides judgment: all of that is Eros's arrow. Ancient psychology described the subjective experience perfectly by making it literal.
facts.visualDescription: Winged boy with a serious expression drawing back a small bow, two quivers at his hip — one gleaming gold, one dull grey — floating above an ordinary city street where people go about their business entirely unaware, one golden arrow already in flight toward a man just turning to look at someone

variants.question: What effect did Eros's lead-tipped arrows have, opposite to his golden arrows?
distractors: They made the target invisible to others

variants.question: Eros is the son of which goddess?
True or false: Eros had both gold-tipped arrows that caused love and lead-tipped arrows that caused hatred.
The Roman name for Eros, god of love, is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god had goat legs and horns, invented the pan flute after a nymph transformed to escape him, and whose name gave us the word 'panic'?
facts.explanation: Pan, son of Hermes, was the god of wild places, flocks, and the primal outdoors. He fell in love with the nymph Syrinx, who fled him and prayed to the river nymphs to be changed. She became hollow marsh reeds. Pan, grieving, cut reeds of different lengths and bound them together, creating the syrinx (pan flute) and played it in her memory. His other attribute — the terrifying, sourceless shout that erupted from wilderness suddenly — caused irrational mass flight, the reflex now called panic. Soldiers in battle, travelers in empty forests, sheep in open fields: the sudden terror without visible cause was Pan's. He was credited at the Battle of Marathon (490 BCE) with panicking the Persian army, and the Athenians built him a shrine in gratitude.
facts.statement: Pan invented his flute from the reeds of a nymph who transformed to escape him — and his sudden shriek from the wilderness became the word 'panic'.
facts.wowFactor: Pan's name is embedded in the English language in two places: panic (the irrational fear without cause) and pandemic (pan = all, demos = people — the fear that spreads to everyone). A goat-legged god of shepherds is etymologically present in the most terrifying word in modern public health. Ancient language choices have very long lives.
facts.visualDescription: Ancient forest at dusk, the god playing reed pipes on a moss-covered rock, goat hooves visible, curved horns catching the last light, and all around him in the trees the animals — deer, boar, fox — not fleeing but frozen in the specific kind of stillness that precedes flight, waiting for the next sound before they decide

variants.question: How did Pan invent the pan flute (syrinx)?
variants.correctAnswer: The nymph Syrinx transformed into reeds to escape him, which he fashioned into a flute
distractors: Hermes gave him a lyre which he converted into pipes
He carved it from the wood of Apollo's sacred laurel tree
The Muses taught him to make it from hollow bones
He found it abandoned in a forest by a river nymph

variants.question: Which word in modern English derives directly from the god Pan?
True or false: Pan's sudden shout could cause irrational terror, which is the origin of the word 'panic'.
The god Pan created the ___ flute after the nymph Syrinx transformed into marsh reeds.

facts.quizQuestion: Which winged goddess of victory flew over battlefields to crown winners, and whose name was borrowed by a famous shoe brand?
facts.explanation: Nike (pronounced NEE-kay in Greek) was the personification of victory, daughter of the Titan Pallas and the Oceanid Styx — two ancient forces combined into the goddess of prevailing. She stood beside Zeus and Athena in battle, descending with golden wings to place laurel wreaths on victors. The Romans called her Victoria. At the 1971 meeting where the athletic shoe company needed a name, a marketing student suggested Nike as a last-minute option — the board accepted it with some uncertainty, and it became the best-known brand name in sport. The winged goddess who crowned Greek athletes at Olympia now graces the feet of athletes at every stadium on earth.
facts.statement: Nike flew over battles and athletic contests to crown the winner — and her name, borrowed by a shoe company in 1971, now appears on billions of pairs of shoes worldwide.
facts.wowFactor: The Nike founder Phil Knight said when the name was suggested he didn't love it but couldn't think of anything better. The student who suggested it said she liked it 'because it has zip.' One Greek goddess of victory, one uncertain board meeting in Oregon, and two billion shoes a year bearing her name.
facts.visualDescription: Golden-winged figure descending steeply from overcast sky over a stadium, laurel wreath in one extended hand, expression of complete focus on the single figure below who has crossed a line first, the other competitors still visible behind, and the crowd in the stone seats rising

variants.question: What was the Roman name for the Greek goddess Nike?
Nike was the daughter of which Titaness associated with oaths?
True or false: Nike was the winged Greek goddess of victory who flew over battlefields to crown winners.
The winged goddess of victory in Greek mythology was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which goddess embodied divine retribution against those who displayed hubris or excessive pride before the gods?
facts.explanation: Nemesis was the goddess of righteous indignation and divine retribution, but her function was more precise than punishment: she balanced. Excessive happiness invited her attention as much as excessive pride — she ensured that nothing in the mortal world remained permanently, disproportionately fortunate. The Persian king Xerxes, at the height of his power before invading Greece, threw golden chains into the Hellespont to symbolize his dominion over the sea — Nemesis noted the hubris. The Greeks who defeated him at Marathon and Thermopylae believed she had been watching. Her name entered English as a synonym for an opponent or downfall because she was the entity that ensured every ascent had a corresponding descent. Narcissus's fate in this sub-deck was specifically attributed to her action.
facts.statement: Nemesis was not a punisher of evil — she was a corrector of imbalance, ensuring no mortal's fortune or pride ever tipped the cosmic scales too far.
facts.wowFactor: Nemesis applied to both luck and pride equally — she was not moral judgment but cosmic arithmetic. A person could invite her attention simply by being too fortunate for too long, even without any arrogance. The universe, in Greek theology, refused to allow permanent imbalance. Every windfall was also a debt.
facts.visualDescription: A figure in dark robes standing at the top of a wheel — half of it rising, half falling — with a measuring rod and a set of scales, her expression not cruel but impartial in the way that mathematics is impartial, looking down at a city that is currently very prosperous and has been for too long

variants.question: What Greek concept did Nemesis primarily punish in mortals?
What does the word 'nemesis' mean in modern English, derived from the goddess?
variants.correctAnswer: A powerful opponent or source of downfall

variants.question: True or false: Nemesis was the Greek goddess of divine retribution who punished excessive pride.
The Greek goddess of divine retribution against hubris was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rainbow goddess served as personal messenger to Hera, just as Hermes served Zeus, and whose name is still used for a part of the eye?
facts.explanation: Where Hermes served Zeus as universal messenger, Iris served Hera specifically — she was the feminine, visual counterpart to the gods' primary herald. She traveled along the rainbow she created, moving between Olympus and the mortal world at impossible speed. Her connection to color was total: she gave her name to the iris of the human eye (the colored ring around the pupil), to the iris flower genus (Linnaeus named it for her range of colors), and to iridescence — the play of spectrum color on surfaces. The scientific word for a prism-produced spectrum is also derived from Iris. One goddess of the rainbow named color science, ophthalmology, and botany.
facts.statement: Iris was the rainbow personified — Hera's personal messenger, traveling at wind-speed along her own colors — and her name survives in the eye, the flower, and the spectrum.
facts.wowFactor: Every time an optometrist mentions your iris, every time a botanist classifies an iris flower, every time a physicist describes iridescence, they are using the vocabulary of a Greek goddess. Iris achieved what few mythological figures managed: she became so associated with a natural phenomenon that her name became the technical term for that phenomenon across multiple scientific disciplines.
facts.visualDescription: Arcing band of color against a dark stormcloud, a winged figure visible within the arc as both its source and its traveler, robes cycling through every color as she moves, a small sealed message container at her belt, the surface of the sea far below catching the same spectrum in broken fragments

variants.question: Which part of the human eye is named after the rainbow goddess Iris?
variants.correctAnswer: The colored ring around the pupil (iris)

variants.question: Iris served as personal messenger to which goddess, while Hermes served Zeus?
True or false: Iris was the goddess of the rainbow and served as Hera's personal messenger.
The rainbow goddess Iris served as personal messenger to ___.

facts.quizQuestion: How many Muses were there in Greek mythology, daughters of Zeus and Mnemosyne (Memory), each ruling a different art or science?
facts.explanation: The nine Muses — Calliope (epic poetry), Clio (history), Erato (love poetry), Euterpe (flute music), Melpomene (tragedy), Polyhymnia (sacred hymns), Terpsichore (dance), Thalia (comedy), and Urania (astronomy) — were daughters of Zeus and the Titaness Mnemosyne (Memory). Their parentage is meaningful: creativity is what happens when divine energy meets the capacity to remember. The Library of Alexandria contained a Mouseion — a place of the Muses — which is the direct etymological ancestor of every museum in the world. Homer invoked the Muses at the opening of the Iliad; the tradition of invoking them continued through Virgil, Dante, Milton, and beyond. Their mother Mnemosyne also gives us 'mnemonic,' and Calliope's story threads directly to Orpheus in this sub-deck.
facts.statement: Nine daughters of Zeus and Memory presided over every art and science — and their place of gathering gave us the word 'museum'.
facts.wowFactor: Every museum in the world is named for these nine goddesses. 'Mouseion' — place of the Muses — was what the Greeks called a site of learning and the arts. When you visit any museum, art gallery, or concert hall, you are entering what the Greeks conceived of as their sacred space. The Muses still own all the buildings.
facts.visualDescription: Rocky hillside at dawn near a sacred spring, nine women in distinct robes arranged in an informal semicircle — one has a scroll, one a small lyre, one a theatrical mask, one measuring stars with a brass armillary — each absorbed in her domain, the spring Hippocrene glittering below them, Helicon's peak catching the first light

variants.question: Which Muse presided over epic poetry and was considered chief among the nine?
Which two modern English words derive from the word for the Muses ('Mousa')?
True or false: There were nine Muses, and the words 'music' and 'museum' both derive from their name.
The Muses were daughters of Zeus and ___, the Titaness of Memory.

facts.quizQuestion: Which of the three Fates (Moirai) spun the thread of life at the moment of a person's birth?
facts.explanation: The Moirai — Fates — were among the oldest powers in Greek cosmology, predating the Olympians and operating beyond their authority. Even Zeus was subject to their decisions, though ancient sources debate whether he could sometimes influence them. Clotho (the Spinner) created the thread of life at a person's birth. Lachesis (the Allotter) measured its length, determining the life's duration. Atropos (she who cannot be turned) cut it at the destined moment. The three Norns of Norse mythology — Urd, Verdandi, and Skuld — perform the same function at Yggdrasil's base, spinning, weaving, and cutting the fates of gods and mortals, making this one of the most universal mythological structures across Indo-European traditions.
facts.statement: Three ancient women controlled every life ever lived: Clotho spun it into existence, Lachesis measured how long it would last, and Atropos ended it without appeal.
facts.wowFactor: Atropos's name — 'she who cannot be turned' — was given to the drug atropine, derived from the deadly nightshade plant Atropa belladonna. In high doses, atropine stops the heart. In clinical doses, it restarts it during cardiac arrest. Doctors use a drug named for the goddess of death's end to reverse death. The Fates are present in every emergency room.
facts.visualDescription: Dim underground chamber, three ancient women at an enormous spinning apparatus that has no visible end — the thread it produces stretches away into darkness in both directions — one spinning, eyes closed, expression of pure function; one measuring with a golden rod along the thread's length, head tilted; one sitting apart, scissors in both hands, waiting, patient as geology

variants.question: Which of the three Fates cut the thread of life at death?
Even which god was considered bound by the decisions of the three Fates?
True or false: The three Fates controlled destiny even over the gods, with Clotho spinning, Lachesis measuring, and Atropos cutting the thread of life.
The Fate who spun the thread of life at a person's birth was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which three snake-haired goddesses pursued and tormented murderers — especially those who killed family members — driving them to madness?
facts.explanation: The Erinyes (Eumenides in polite Greek, Furies in Latin) — Alecto, Megaera, and Tisiphone — were born from the blood of Uranus when Kronos castrated him, making them older than the Olympians and more fundamentally connected to blood-crime and its consequences. They were not judges — they did not evaluate guilt — they were punishments automated: once blood-guilt existed, the Furies arrived and did not stop. Orestes, who killed his mother Clytemnestra to avenge his father Agamemnon (a story threading from the Trojan War sub-deck), was pursued relentlessly even though Athena ultimately ruled his case justified. The Greeks' avoidance of their name — calling them 'the Kindly Ones' to avoid triggering them — is the original superstitious euphemism.
facts.statement: The Furies pursued murderers — especially family killers — with torments that never ceased, driving them to madness, and were so feared the Greeks called them 'the Kindly Ones' to avoid saying the real name.
facts.wowFactor: The Greeks were so frightened of the Furies that they developed a religious etiquette for not summoning them: use the euphemism, never say the real name, never claim their attention unnecessarily. The Furies were the mechanism by which the universe guaranteed that certain acts would not go unanswered — which made them, in a way, the most reliable force in all of Greek theology.
facts.visualDescription: Night landscape, rocky and without landmarks, a lone figure running in absolute silence — and behind him, not running but simply there, closing the distance by existing closer each time he looks back, three winged shapes with snakes where hair should be, carrying torches that burn black, their faces expressions of absolute purpose rather than rage

variants.question: What crime did the Furies specifically and most relentlessly punish?
Why did Greeks call the Furies 'Eumenides' (the Kindly Ones)?
variants.correctAnswer: To avoid saying their real name and angering them
distractors: Because they could also grant mercy to the truly repentant
Because Athena reformed them into benevolent spirits
Because they were daughters of Gaia

variants.question: True or false: The Furies had snakes for hair and pursued murderers — especially those who killed family members — driving them to madness.
The snake-haired goddesses who punished murderers were called the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Jason and the Argonauts sailed to Colchis to retrieve what sacred object — the wool of a divine ram, hung in a grove guarded by a sleepless dragon?
facts.explanation: The Golden Fleece was the wool of Chrysomallus, the divine ram who had carried Phrixus safely from Greece to Colchis. After the ram was sacrificed to Zeus, its fleece was hung in the sacred grove of Ares in Colchis — modern Georgia — guarded by an enormous dragon that never slept. It represented divine authority and kingship. Pelias had been told by an oracle to fear 'a man wearing one sandal' — Jason arrived in Iolcos having lost a sandal crossing a river — so Pelias sent him on the Fleece quest as a death sentence. Medea's role in making the quest possible is detailed in myth_jason_medea_tasks and myth_medea_jason_fleece in this sub-deck. Some ancient historians believed the Golden Fleece had a practical origin: Colchian gold miners used sheepskins in rivers to catch gold dust, producing fleeces that gleamed with real gold.
facts.statement: The Golden Fleece was a sacred golden ram's wool hung in a grove guarded by a sleepless dragon — and King Pelias sent Jason for it expecting him to die.
facts.wowFactor: Some archaeologists believe the Golden Fleece is a real object — ancient Colchian miners placed sheepskins in mountain streams to trap gold dust particles in the fleece fibers. A golden, heavy sheepskin, dripping gold, hung in a temple grove: that is an actual historical object that may have inspired the myth. The metaphor was real.
facts.visualDescription: Ancient sacred grove at midnight, silver moonlight through old branches, a great gold sheepskin hanging from the largest oak glowing faintly with its own light, and below it on the mossy ground a serpent coiled so many times the ground beneath it is invisible, one eye that has never closed in the history of the world half-open and reflecting the gold above

variants.question: Where was the Golden Fleece kept, guarded by a dragon that never slept?
variants.correctAnswer: A sacred grove of Ares in Colchis
distractors: The temple of Zeus at Olympia
The throne room of King Pelias

variants.question: How did Jason get past the sleepless dragon guarding the Golden Fleece?
variants.correctAnswer: Medea charmed it with her sorcery
distractors: He used a mirror like Perseus did against Medusa
He drowned it with Poseidon's help
He distracted it with golden apples

variants.question: True or false: The Golden Fleece was the wool of a divine ram, guarded by a dragon in Colchis.
Jason and the Argonauts sailed to ___ to retrieve the Golden Fleece.

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the first mortal woman, created by the gods, whose curiosity led her to open a jar (mistranslated as 'box') releasing all the world's evils?
facts.explanation: The pithos (large storage jar) in Pandora's myth is a different object from the pyxis (small cosmetics box) that Erasmus confused it with in 1508. A pithos was a person-sized clay container used to store grain, oil, or wine — large, sealed with wax, kept in domestic storerooms. The evils sealed inside were abstract personifications: Geras (old age), Oizys (misery), Momos (blame), Ponos (hardship), Moros (doom), and Thanatos (death), among others. What remained inside was Elpis — Hope — which some ancient commentators read as humanity's greatest gift (we still have it) and others as Zeus's final cruelty (he kept the one thing that makes suffering bearable locked away, ours only because we cannot access it fully). The jar as vessel threads through this sub-deck to Cornucopia (another vessel of consequence) and back to Prometheus's theft in the Titans & Origins sub-deck.
facts.statement: Pandora's jar — never a box, the word 'box' is a 500-year-old translator's error — held all the world's evils, and only Hope was left inside when she closed it.
facts.wowFactor: For over 500 years the most famous container in world mythology has been misnamed. Every 'Pandora's box' reference in literature, law, politics, and popular culture since 1508 has been technically wrong. One confused scholar in the Renaissance renamed the object at the center of the myth of evil's origin. The mistranslation won.
facts.visualDescription: Large clay storage jar, the height of a standing woman, sealed at the top with beeswax that is now cracked in a ring where hands have been — all around the jar the air is disturbed, the quality of light is wrong, something has been here and is now not contained anymore — and inside the jar, visible through the crack at the top, a single point of warm light that has not yet escaped

variants.question: What was the one thing that remained in Pandora's jar after all the evils escaped?
Why was Pandora created and sent to humanity, according to Zeus?
variants.correctAnswer: As punishment for Prometheus stealing fire from the gods
distractors: To reward Epimetheus for his loyalty
To teach mortals the value of wisdom
To spread the gods' gifts among humanity
To test whether mortals could resist temptation

variants.question: True or false: 'Pandora's Box' is technically a mistranslation — the original Greek word was 'pithos,' meaning a large jar.
After Pandora's jar released all evils, only ___ remained inside.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the divine shield — sometimes bearing Medusa's head — shared by Zeus and Athena, whose name gives us the phrase 'under the aegis of'?
facts.explanation: The aegis's origins vary by source: in some accounts it is the skin of the divine goat Amalthea (see myth_object_cornucopia in this sub-deck, whose horn came from the same goat), in others a divine breastplate made by Hephaestus, in others a terrifying fringed cloak that caused fear in enemies. What is consistent is Medusa's face at its center — the Gorgoneion. Perseus killed Medusa using a mirror-shield Athena provided (myth_medusa_perseus in the Monsters sub-deck), then gave Athena the severed head as a gift. Athena mounted it on her own shield. The goddess who cursed Medusa provided the weapon to kill her, then wore her face. 'Under the aegis of' still means under the protection or sponsorship of a powerful force.
facts.statement: The aegis was Athena's divine shield-cloak, bearing Medusa's head at its center — a weapon made from the face of the goddess's own victim.
facts.wowFactor: Athena cursed Medusa for being assaulted in her temple. She then gave Perseus the tool to kill her. She then wore Medusa's face on her shield as a weapon. The entire arc — curse, assassination, display — was Athena's. Medusa's story is wholly contained within Athena's authority over it, from beginning to bronze.
facts.visualDescription: The shield propped upright against a marble column, celestial metal at the rim, and at the center a woman's face — eyes open and fixed, radiating something that makes the stone floor in front of it lighten and crack, small as stone chips, snakes at the face's edge still faintly moving, Athena's spear visible leaning against the wall beside it

variants.question: What does the modern phrase 'under the aegis of' mean, derived from the divine shield?
variants.correctAnswer: Under the protection or sponsorship of

variants.question: Whose severed head was mounted on Athena's aegis to terrify enemies?
True or false: The aegis was a divine shield associated with both Zeus and Athena, and Athena's version bore Medusa's head.
The divine shield called the aegis was associated with Zeus and ___.

facts.quizQuestion: What 'horn of plenty' — originally a horn broken from the goat Amalthea who nursed baby Zeus — magically overflowed with food and drink without end?
facts.explanation: While Zeus was hidden in Crete from his father Kronos (who was consuming his children), the divine goat Amalthea nursed him. Zeus accidentally broke one of her horns during play. To compensate, he blessed the broken horn: it would overflow endlessly with whatever food, drink, or flowers the holder desired. This became the cornucopia — 'horn of plenty,' from Latin cornu (horn) and copia (abundance). The same Amalthea appears in the aegis myth (myth_object_aegis in this sub-deck), where her skin becomes Athena's divine shield. One goat contributed both the symbol of abundance and the armor of wisdom to Greek mythology, which is more significant a single animal career than most gods managed.
facts.statement: Zeus broke the nursing goat's horn by accident and blessed it to overflow with whatever the holder desired — the first act of divine generosity from the hidden infant god.
facts.wowFactor: Zeus, as a hidden infant in a cave fleeing a father who had already eaten his five siblings, accidentally created the permanent symbol of abundance by breaking a goat's horn. His very first divine act was compensatory generosity from a position of total vulnerability. The cornucopia is still on every Thanksgiving table.
facts.visualDescription: Large curved horn resting on a stone surface, its open end pointed slightly upward and pouring — not with the drama of a fountain but with the easy permanence of something that has always been pouring and will never stop — grapes, pomegranates, figs, small birds, wheat, and between these objects an inexplicable golden light that does not belong to any of them individually

variants.question: Which goat nursed the infant Zeus and whose horn became the cornucopia?
Where was baby Zeus hidden while Amalthea nursed him, keeping him safe from Kronos?
True or false: The cornucopia originated from the horn of the goat Amalthea, which Zeus accidentally broke and blessed to overflow with abundance.
The cornucopia was originally the horn of the divine goat ___.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the food of the Greek gods, counterpart to their divine drink nectar, which granted immortality to those who consumed it?
facts.explanation: Ambrosia and nectar were the complementary pair of divine sustenance: ambrosia the food, nectar the drink, both necessary to maintain the gods' immortality. The gods' blood was also distinctive — ichor rather than blood, flowing gold rather than red, which is why a wounded god did not bleed but 'ichor ran.' Occasionally a god would share ambrosia with a favored mortal to grant divine status: Ganymede was given it when Zeus made him cupbearer of the gods, and Psyche drank it when Zeus made her immortal in this sub-deck. The word 'ambrosia' entered English as a poetic term for anything delicious or divinely pleasurable, which is precisely how the Greeks used it: the taste of immortality, whatever that would be.
facts.statement: Ambrosia was the food that made the gods immortal — and the word literally means 'belonging to the immortals' in Greek, which is still how we use it.
facts.wowFactor: The word 'ambrosia' means 'immortal' and we still use it as a word for anything delicious — which means every time someone says 'this food is ambrosial,' they are saying it tastes like something that could give you eternal life. Ancient vocabulary choices do not lose their theological payload, they just lose their theology.
facts.visualDescription: A golden bowl the size of a cupped palm, on a white stone table somewhere above cloud level, containing something that glows with its own faint light the color of honey at high noon — not liquid, not solid — and beside it a golden cup of nectar whose color is wrong in the way that divine things are wrong: too saturated, too real

variants.question: What was the divine drink of the gods, paired with ambrosia the food?
What substance flowed in the veins of the Greek gods instead of blood?
True or false: Ambrosia was the food of the Greek gods that granted immortality, paired with nectar as their drink.
The food of the Greek gods that granted immortality was called ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Muse presided over epic poetry and was considered the greatest of the nine — also the mother of Orpheus?
facts.explanation: Calliope ('beautiful voice') presided over epic poetry and was considered the most honored of the Muses. She was the mother of Orpheus by Apollo — meaning the greatest musician in mythology was the son of the god of music and the Muse of the grandest literary form. His gift was not metaphorically divine but literally genetic: both parents were divine forces connected to music and language. Homer invoked Calliope at the opening of the Iliad and Odyssey; Virgil invoked her in the Aeneid; the tradition of addressing the Muse at a poem's opening comes directly from the belief that epic poetry required her active participation to succeed. Her son's story runs through multiple entries in this sub-deck — myth_orpheus_eurydice, myth_orpheus_hades_deal, myth_orpheus_maenads — as the primary narrative arc of Love, Tragedy & Fate.
facts.statement: Calliope, chief of the nine Muses, was the goddess of epic poetry — and Orpheus's extraordinary gift for music descended from her, through divine parentage on both sides.
facts.wowFactor: Orpheus was the son of the god of music and the Muse of epic poetry. His talent was not a gift or a skill — it was his nature, both sides of his parentage demanding that music flow through him. The tragedy of his story is that nature's most perfect musician was destroyed not by a monster or a god but by doubt and grief.
facts.visualDescription: A woman with an expression of absolute creative authority — not stern but charged, the way the air is charged before lightning — seated at the top of a hillside with a golden writing tablet, the other Muses at their own distances below her, and beside her a lyre resting untouched that seems to be vibrating very slightly on its own, as if remembering someone who is not here

variants.question: Calliope, the chief Muse, was the mother of which legendary musician?
Epic poets like Homer traditionally began their poems by invoking which Muse?
True or false: Calliope was the chief Muse, presiding over epic poetry, and was the mother of Orpheus.
The chief of the nine Muses, who presided over epic poetry, was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Muse presided over tragedy and is depicted holding a tragic mask, while her counterpart Thalia held a comic mask?
facts.explanation: Melpomene ('to sing') was the Muse of tragedy, depicted with a tragic theatrical mask and the high-soled boots (cothurnus) that tragic actors wore to command attention in the large stone theaters of Athens. Thalia ('the festive') was the Muse of comedy, holding a comic mask and a shepherd's crook. Together their masks represent not just two theatrical genres but two fundamental human orientations toward experience: the tragic, which insists on taking suffering seriously, and the comic, which refuses to. The paired masks appear on theater buildings, in drama awards, on acting school logos, and in every theatrical context worldwide — an unbroken visual tradition from 5th-century Athens through the present day.
facts.statement: Melpomene and Thalia — the Muses of tragedy and comedy — gave theater its two masks, which have been the universal symbol of drama for 2,500 unbroken years.
facts.wowFactor: The two faces on every theater, every drama department, every acting award trophy in the world are Melpomene and Thalia — two specific Greek goddesses. This symbol has not needed updating or explaining in 2,500 years. No other field of human activity uses the same unbroken symbol across 25 centuries of change.
facts.visualDescription: Two Muses on the curved stone steps of a Greek theater at dusk, the stone seats beginning to fill with an audience behind them — one in deep blue holding a weeping face forward at arm's length, head tilted slightly, the expression of someone who finds suffering necessary; one in saffron yellow holding a laughing face with the easy posture of someone who knows the joke is coming

variants.question: Which Muse was the counterpart of Melpomene, presiding over comedy?
The paired comedy and tragedy masks seen on theaters worldwide represent which two Muses?
True or false: Melpomene was the Muse of tragedy and Thalia the Muse of comedy, and their masks became the universal symbol of theater.
The Muse who presided over tragedy and held the weeping mask was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which of the three Fates cut the thread of life at death, and whose name — meaning she who cannot be turned — was given to the drug atropine?
facts.explanation: Atropos — 'she who cannot be turned' — was the most feared of the three Fates because she was the terminal one. Where Clotho and Lachesis created and measured, Atropos ended. Not even Zeus could redirect her scissors. The drug atropine, extracted from the deadly nightshade plant Atropa belladonna, takes its genus name from her: in high doses it stops the heart, performing her function; in clinical doses it is used to restart stopped hearts during cardiac arrest, inverting her function. Belladonna ('beautiful woman') was named for a separate reason — women used diluted nightshade drops to dilate their pupils, considered fashionable. The plant was named for vanity; its active compound was named for the goddess of death's end. The nomenclature tells a complete story.
facts.statement: Atropos cut the thread of life and could not be persuaded by anyone — and her name was given to atropine, a drug that can both stop and restart the heart.
facts.wowFactor: Atropine restarts hearts in emergency medicine — but its source plant, Atropa belladonna, is named for the goddess who ends lives. Every cardiac emergency doctor who uses atropine is reaching for a drug named for the entity whose function they are temporarily reversing. The Fates are in the crash cart.
facts.visualDescription: A single white thread stretched horizontally across the frame in perfect stillness, and above it a pair of ancient bronze scissors held open, not moving, not yet moving — and behind the scissors, barely resolved in the dark, a figure of complete patience, expression not cruel, not hurried, simply fixed, waiting for the exact right moment that only she knows

variants.question: What does the name Atropos mean, reflecting her role among the Fates?
Which medical drug takes its name from Atropos via the plant Atropa belladonna?
True or false: Atropos was the Fate who cut the thread of life, and her name was given to the medical drug atropine.
The Fate who cut the thread of life and could not be turned aside was ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which of Oedipus’s daughters guided him in his blind exile, walking with him across Greece after he was cast out of Thebes?
facts.explanation: Oedipus's sons Eteocles and Polyneices, who would later kill each other in civil war (myth_antigone_burial in this sub-deck), refused to accompany their disgraced father into exile. His daughter Antigone did not refuse. She walked beside him for years, guiding him by the hand, until they reached Colonus near Athens, where King Theseus received them with hospitality. Oedipus died there peacefully — his grave, according to Sophocles, becoming a sacred protective site for Athens. Antigone then returned to Thebes, where the conflict over her brother's burial was waiting for her. Her entire story is a continuous arc of loyalty: to her father against political judgment, to her brother against state law, each time accepting the consequence without retreating.
facts.statement: After Oedipus blinded himself and was exiled from Thebes, only Antigone chose to go with him — walking with a blind, broken king across the Greek world until his death.
facts.wowFactor: Antigone chose her blind, cursed, exiled father when his own sons did not. She then returned to Thebes and chose her dead brother when the law forbade it. In a mythology full of heroes who chose glory, Antigone repeatedly chose the person in front of her. Greek tragedy produced no quieter, more persistent form of courage.
facts.visualDescription: Dusty road between mountains, a young woman walking with one hand lightly on the arm of an old man in torn royal clothing, his other hand extended slightly forward into empty air, both moving at the same pace through a landscape that does not care about them, a city visible in the far distance on the plain they are leaving

variants.question: At which place near Athens did the exiled Oedipus finally die peacefully, guided by Antigone?
Which Athenian king received the blind, exiled Oedipus with hospitality at Colonus?
True or false: After Oedipus was blinded and exiled, his daughter Antigone guided him in his exile until his death.
After Oedipus’s exile, his daughter ___ served as his guide and companion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which cursed nymph could only repeat others’ words, fell in love with Narcissus, and faded away until only her voice remained?
facts.explanation: Echo's curse (myth_echo_curse in this sub-deck) and Narcissus's fate intersected in a single forest: she could say only what she had just heard, and he had been made to love the one face no one else could give him. When Narcissus called into the woods 'Is anyone here?' Echo could only respond 'Here!' When he called 'Come!' she rushed toward him and was rejected with cold disgust. She faded away; Narcissus moved on, to find his pool. Two curses, two prison sentences, two people who could not connect with another human being — overlapping in the same place at the same time. Their story as a pair is mythology's most precise portrait of mutual loneliness: not enemies, not strangers, but two people simultaneously incapable of meeting each other.
facts.statement: Echo could only repeat his words. Narcissus could only love his own face. They met in the same forest and were equally unable to reach another person.
facts.wowFactor: Echo and Narcissus are not a love story — they are two parallel studies in the same problem: the inability to exist outside oneself. She can only echo. He can only reflect. Put them together and you get the mythological image of two people in the same conversation, neither of whom is actually communicating. The word 'echo' describing a reflected sound comes from this collision of two inward-facing failures.
facts.visualDescription: Forest mid-morning, sun through trees, a beautiful young man walking forward without looking back, and behind him at the treeline a figure barely visible — translucent except for the mouth, which is still clearly a mouth, still open, still forming the last words he said back to him, his footsteps already reaching the pool ahead

variants.question: What physical phenomenon takes its name from the nymph who loved Narcissus?
How did Echo try to confess her love to Narcissus despite Hera’s curse?
variants.correctAnswer: By repeating his own words back to him as an invitation
distractors: By leaving flowers on his path
By writing his name on trees
By singing to him at night

variants.question: True or false: Echo faded away from grief until only her voice remained, explaining why a reflected sound is called an echo.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Cypriot sculptor was so disgusted by real women’s flaws that he created an ivory statue of his ideal and fell in love with it?
facts.explanation: In Ovid's Metamorphoses, Pygmalion's misogyny had a specific trigger: he had seen the Propoetides — women of Amathus who denied the gods' divinity and were punished by being transformed into prostitutes, their faces hardened to stone. Revolted, he resolved never to love a woman and channeled everything into art: carving from white elephant ivory a figure of his private ideal. He then fell in love with what he had made, giving it gifts, clothing it, addressing it, sleeping beside it. The irony is structural: he made the perfect woman by removing everything in real women that was not his preference. He then had to pray to Aphrodite — the goddess of love, whose power he had been trying to route around — to make his preference real. She did, and he lost control of his creation immediately, because Galatea was now a real person with her own will.
facts.statement: Pygmalion rejected real women and carved instead the perfect woman from ivory — falling in love with his own ideal, the one thing he would never have to compromise with.
facts.wowFactor: Pygmalion spent years loving a version of a person that could not disagree, need things, be tired, or be disappointed. Then Aphrodite made it real and gave it all those things. His story is the origin of every myth about preferring an idealized image to a real person — which means it is the oldest description of a very specific modern problem.
facts.visualDescription: Sculptor's workshop, the abandoned tools and the one finished thing on its pedestal: a full-length ivory figure with perfect proportions and a blank expression, and the sculptor at a workbench some distance away not looking at it, but positioned in exactly the direction he would look if he turned — having arranged the room so that wherever he sits, she is in the corner of his eye

variants.question: What inspired Pygmalion to create the perfect ivory statue rather than pursue a real relationship?
variants.correctAnswer: Disgust at the moral failings of real women
distractors: Grief after a real lover died
A prophecy that his statue would come to life

variants.question: In which Roman author’s Metamorphoses is the most famous version of the Pygmalion story told?
True or false: Pygmalion carved a perfect woman from ivory after becoming disgusted with real women, then fell in love with his creation.
The Pygmalion Effect in modern psychology is named after a sculptor who fell in love with his own ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which sorceress helped Jason complete three impossible tasks to win the Golden Fleece — including charming a dragon and making Jason invulnerable to fire?
facts.explanation: Medea's lineage positioned her at the intersection of solar divine power (Helios as grandfather) and underworld magical authority (Hecate as patron goddess). Her fireproofing ointment, made from the crocus that grew where Prometheus's blood fell on Mount Caucasus, made Jason immune to the fire-breathing bulls for one day — the exact duration of the trial. Her tactical advice about the Spartoi (dragon-tooth warriors) saved him from his own limited military imagination. Her lullaby to the sleepless dragon was the act that made the Fleece accessible at all. The quest named after Jason was won by Medea three times over. Her subsequent story — betrayal, revenge, escape — fills myth_medea_revenge in this sub-deck, and together the two entries form the complete arc of what Greek tragedy does with a woman who is more capable than the hero she serves.
facts.statement: Medea was a granddaughter of the sun and a priestess of Hecate — and she used her full divine inheritance to win Jason a Fleece he could not have touched without her.
facts.wowFactor: Jason is celebrated. His quest is named after him. The ship is named the Argo, after its builder. The heroes are the Argonauts, after the ship. Medea is present in none of these names and is the reason any of them succeeded. Greek mythology gave the glory to the man who walked through the door she had already opened.
facts.visualDescription: Dawn over the grove of Ares in Colchis: a woman standing very still beside a massive sleeping dragon, one hand raised in a gesture that is not threatening but commanding, and from her palm a sound is emerging that is visible — not light, something between sound and smoke — while the dragon's eyes, which have not closed in the memory of anyone alive, are closed

variants.question: What protective gift did Medea give Jason that made him immune to fire and iron?
Which goddess did Medea serve as a priestess, the source of much of her magical power?
True or false: Without Medea’s magical help, Jason would not have been able to complete the tasks to win the Golden Fleece.
